# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
ok, i dont know how to phrase it, but the vision is something like, gunmetal being a control/command centre for your inference usage. basically, like how a toll is on the highway. 

our current architecture is great. but i think we can simlify it even more to my vision. 

overall, i thought of 3 things (products):

- api console - the current implementation, where the users can add their providers and create api keys to route to them. this is for consumers, where they wanna use already existing subscriptions for inference in other apps they use which require api keys. 

- extention store - current implementation of our providers is modular design, where we can add as many as we want. i want to just package it differently. basically, a extention store, which will have all the providers as extentions. this extention store is going to be like a sdk or something. it is for devs who are building their software and wanna add multiple proivder support. it is very similar in terms of goal to pi-ai : https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/tree/main/packages/ai

- toll booth - if you see, gunmetal is positioned very well, it sits b/w the upstream provider giving the inference, and the user/dev who is going to utilize that inference. we can do a lot of great things by just being the middleman. things like, stats, such as token usgae, tps, etc. a lot of opportunities. 


note that we wont be making any other repo/app/etc, we will just refactor gunmetal. 
your thoughts.

ok, i dont know how to phrase it, but the vision is something like, gunmetal being a control/command centre for your inference usage. basically, like how a toll is on the highway. 

our current architecture is great. but i think we can simlify it even more to my vision. 

overall, i thought of 3 things (products):

- api console - the current implementation, where the users can add their providers and create api keys to route to them. this is for consumers, where they wanna use already existing subscriptions for inference in other apps they use which require api keys. 

- extention store - current implementation of our providers is modular design, where we can add as many as we want. i want to just package it differently. basically, a extention store, which will have all the providers as extentions. this extention store is going to be like a sdk or something. it is for devs who are building their software and wanna add multiple proivder support. it is very similar in terms of goal to pi-ai : https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/tree/main/packages/ai

- toll booth - if you see, gunmetal is positioned very well, it sits b/w the upstream provider giving the inference, and the user/dev who is going to utilize that inference. we can do a lot of great things by just being the middleman. things like, stats, such as token usgae, tps, etc. a lot of opportunities. 


note that we wont be making any other repo/app/etc, we will just refactor gunmetal. 
your thoughts.
I’m grounding this in the durable-memory baseline first, then I’ll reframe your three product ideas from first principles and call out where the current repo helps versus where it’s baggage.

# Prompt

## Task
- Establish durable memory for `gunmetal` before follow-on work. Inspect the current codebase and git history, capture the product vision and present architecture, and seed the required `docs/` files so future tasks can use them as the source of truth.

## Core Goal
- Create a concise, current durable-memory baseline for the project and this prerequisite phase.

## Goals
- Capture the product shape, workspace structure, and main runtime surfaces as they exist on `main`.
- Summarize the major implementation and release history visible in git.
- Record the commands and checks used to inspect and verify the repo state.
- Create `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/implement.md`, and `docs/documentation.md`.

## Non-Goals
- Do not change product behavior, APIs, UI, or release metadata.
- Do not rewrite repository docs outside the new durable-memory set unless later work requires it.
- Do not infer roadmap details that are not supported by the current codebase or git history.

## Constraints
- Be concise.
- Verify the work before reporting back when possible.
- Use the durable-memory files as the source of truth for subsequent work in this repo.
- Keep the docs grounded in the actual repository state, not generic project summaries.

## Deliverables
- `docs/prompt.md`
- `docs/plans.md`
- `docs/implement.md`
- `docs/documentation.md`

## Process Requirements
- Read repo guidance and local durable-memory guidance before substantial work.
- Inspect the current workspace and git history before writing the docs.
- Keep the docs current if scope changes in later work.

## Done When
- All four durable-memory files exist in `docs/`.
- The docs reflect the current product architecture and visible work history.
- The docs record how this prerequisite pass was verified.

# Plans

This document is the execution plan and ongoing notes for the current task. Keep it current as the work moves.

## Verification Checklist
- `test -f docs/prompt.md`
- `test -f docs/plans.md`
- `test -f docs/implement.md`
- `test -f docs/documentation.md`
- Review the new docs with `sed -n '1,220p' docs/*.md`
- Last verified: 2026-04-12

## Milestones
- `[done]` Milestone 1: inspect current repo state and history
  - Scope: repo guidance, workspace shape, product surfaces, and git progress history
  - Key areas: `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, root workspace manifests, `apps/cli`, `apps/web`, `packages/*`, git log/tags
  - Acceptance criteria: current architecture and project arc are understood well enough to write durable memory without guessing
  - Verification steps: `git status --short --branch`, `git log --reverse --oneline --no-decorate`, targeted file reads
- `[done]` Milestone 2: create the durable-memory files
  - Scope: author the required `docs/` set for this prerequisite phase
  - Key areas: `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/implement.md`, `docs/documentation.md`
  - Acceptance criteria: each file is present, concise, and aligned with the current repo state
  - Verification steps: existence checks plus direct file review
- `[done]` Milestone 3: verify consistency
  - Scope: confirm the docs agree with the inspected repo state and task scope
  - Key areas: all new `docs/*.md`
  - Acceptance criteria: no missing file, stale placeholder text, or contradiction with the inspected code/history
  - Verification steps: re-read the files and compare against the gathered repo context

## Acceptance Checks
- The durable-memory set exists and is readable.
- The prompt describes this prerequisite task rather than a generic repo summary.
- The documentation records the current product, repo layout, release/history arc, and next-step posture.

## Validation
- `git status --short --branch`
- `git log --oneline --decorate --graph --all -n 80`
- `git log --reverse --oneline --no-decorate`
- `git rev-list --count --all`
- `git tag --sort=creatordate`
- `find apps packages -maxdepth 2 -type f | sort`
- targeted `sed -n` reads across root docs and key source files
- `test -f docs/prompt.md && test -f docs/plans.md && test -f docs/implement.md && test -f docs/documentation.md`

## Decisions
- Durable memory is initialized around the current prerequisite task: establish project baseline and context before future work.
- No product code changes are part of this pass.
- Future tasks in this repo should update these files first when scope changes materially.

## Implementation Notes
- No prior `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/implement.md`, or `docs/documentation.md` existed at the repo root before this pass.
- Current branch for this work: `docs/durable-memory-init`.
- Current visible release line: `v0.1.0` through `v0.1.8`.
- Current visible commit count across refs: 103.
- Recent arc after the initial build-out is mostly release hardening, web UI parity, and acceptance-loop stability.

## Risks
- Risk: the memory set becomes stale after the next task.
  - Mitigation: update `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, and `docs/documentation.md` before and during follow-on work.
- Risk: history summary hides nuance from individual fixes.
  - Mitigation: use git history directly when a later task depends on a specific regression or subsystem.

## Architecture
- Product: local-first AI switchboard that exposes an OpenAI-compatible local API at `http://127.0.0.1:4684/v1`.
- Runtime surfaces: Rust CLI, Rust TUI, Rust daemon/browser control plane, Next.js marketing/docs site, npm install wrapper.
- Core storage: local files under `~/.gunmetal`, with SQLite state, runtime files, helper directory, and logs.
- Provider model: explicit adapters for `codex`, `copilot`, `openrouter`, `zen`, and `openai`, with additional enum slots for `azure`, `nvidia`, and custom providers.

# Documentation

This document is updated continuously as milestones land so it reflects reality.

## What This Work Is
- This pass initializes durable memory for `gunmetal` before any follow-on implementation. It captures the current product shape, repository layout, work-history arc, and the operating rules future tasks should follow.

## Current Status
- Durable memory is now initialized at the repo root in `docs/`.
- The current product is a local-first AI switchboard: one local OpenAI-compatible API, one local browser control plane, one CLI, and one TUI over shared local state.
- Current branch for this docs work: `docs/durable-memory-init`.

## Status By Milestone
- Milestone 1 complete: repo guidance, workspace shape, and git history reviewed.
- Milestone 2 complete: durable-memory docs created.
- Milestone 3 complete: docs reviewed for consistency against the inspected repo state.

## Setup And Verification
- Repo state:
  - `git status --short --branch`
  - `git log --oneline --decorate --graph --all -n 80`
  - `git log --reverse --oneline --no-decorate`
  - `git rev-list --count --all`
  - `git tag --sort=creatordate`
- Workspace inspection:
  - `find apps packages -maxdepth 2 -type f | sort`
  - targeted `sed -n` reads of `README.md`, workspace manifests, and key source files
- Durable-memory verification:
  - `test -f docs/prompt.md && test -f docs/plans.md && test -f docs/implement.md && test -f docs/documentation.md`
  - direct review of the new docs

## Completed Work
- Captured the current repo organization:
  - `apps/cli`: Rust entrypoint that boots the daemon for default/TUI usage or dispatches CLI commands.
  - `apps/web`: Next.js hosted front door with install/docs/changelog/web-ui pages.
  - `packages/cli`: command parsing and operational flows such as setup, web, start, status, profiles, auth, models, keys, and logs.
  - `packages/daemon`: local HTTP service exposing `/health`, browser control-plane endpoints under `/app/api/*`, and `/v1/models`, `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/responses`.
  - `packages/providers`: provider adapters and registry/hub logic.
  - `packages/storage`: local app-path resolution and SQLite-backed persistence.
  - `packages/core`: shared domain types and API contracts.
  - `packages/tui`: terminal dashboard/operator surface.
  - `packages/npm`: npm distribution wrapper that installs the native binary.
- Captured the visible work-history arc from git:
  - foundation and bootstrap
  - provider rollout for codex, copilot, openrouter, zen, and openai
  - responses API support
  - first-run and TUI hardening
  - monorepo restructure
  - npm packaging and release pipeline
  - hosted site plus local browser UI
  - several release/CI/acceptance stabilization cycles
  - latest visible release tag: `v0.1.8`

## Validation Results
- The repo was clean before this task except for branch state.
- The new durable-memory files now exist in `docs/`.
- The docs match the inspected workspace and history and do not claim unverified roadmap work.

## Decisions
- Use the durable-memory set for future project work in this repo.
- Keep the memory anchored to the current task and actual repo state instead of turning it into generic project notes.
- Treat git history as the source for fine-grained progress details when later work needs subsystem-level context.

## Next Steps
- Start the next requested task using `docs/prompt.md` and `docs/plans.md` as the baseline.
- Refresh the durable-memory files at the start of the next substantive task so the active scope is explicit.

## Follow-Ups
- If the next task targets `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` before making web changes.
- If the next task depends on a specific regression or release, inspect the relevant commits directly instead of relying only on this summary.

  - `packages/daemon`: local HTTP service exposing `/health`, browser control-plane endpoints under `/app/api/*`, and `/v1/models`, `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/responses`.
  - `packages/providers`: provider adapters and registry/hub logic.
  - `packages/storage`: local app-path resolution and SQLite-backed persistence.
  - `packages/core`: shared domain types and API contracts.
  - `packages/tui`: terminal dashboard/operator surface.
  - `packages/npm`: npm distribution wrapper that installs the native binary.
- Captured the visible work-history arc from git:
  - foundation and bootstrap
  - provider rollout for codex, copilot, openrouter, zen, and openai
  - responses API support
  - first-run and TUI hardening
  - monorepo restructure
  - npm packaging and release pipeline
  - hosted site plus local browser UI
  - several release/CI/acceptance stabilization cycles
  - latest visible release tag: `v0.1.8`

## Validation Results
- The repo was clean before this task except for branch state.
- The new durable-memory files now exist in `docs/`.
- The docs match the inspected workspace and history and do not claim unverified roadmap work.

## Decisions
- Use the durable-memory set for future project work in this repo.
- Keep the memory anchored to the current task and actual repo state instead of turning it into generic project notes.
- Treat git history as the source for fine-grained progress details when later work needs subsystem-level context.

## Next Steps
- Start the next requested task using `docs/prompt.md` and `docs/plans.md` as the baseline.
- Refresh the durable-memory files at the start of the next substantive task so the active scope is explicit.

## Follow-Ups
- If the next task targets `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` before making web changes.
- If the next task depends on a specific regression or release, inspect the relevant commits directly instead of relying only on this summary.

payload.message:
My read: you do not have 3 products. You have 1 product with 3 surfaces.

The core product is:

`Gunmetal = the inference control plane between apps and model providers.`

Everything else is packaging.

**Reframe**
- `API console` is the end-user surface of the control plane.
- `Extension store` is the provider integration system for developers.
- `Toll booth` is the economic/observability layer of the same control plane.

That framing is much stronger than “local-first API switchboard,” because it answers why Gunmetal exists: not just to proxy requests, but to govern inference usage.

**What feels right**
- Your “toll booth” metaphor is the best one. It implies every request passes through one checkpoint where you can observe, enrich, route, meter, block, cache, attribute, and bill.
- The current modular provider architecture is not a side idea. It is the foundation of the platform.
- The current API-console product is real, but it is probably only the first wedge, not the whole company.

**What I would challenge**
- An “extension store” is not a product unless there is a painful integration problem for developers that Gunmetal uniquely solves.
- “Stats” alone are not enough. Observability is useful, but not a company. The value is control: routing, budgets, limits, usage attribution, org policies, fallback, caching, and eventually optimization.
- If you pitch all three equally, the product becomes blurry. You need one center of gravity.

**First-principles view**
The real job to be done is probably:

- For consumers: “let me use all my existing AI access from one place in apps that expect API keys.”
- For developers: “let me integrate many providers once, with a stable contract.”
- For teams: “let me see and control who is spending what on inference.”

Those are related, but they are not equal. One must be primary.

**My recommendation**
Make the canonical product:

- `Gunmetal Core`: inference control plane
- `Gunmetal Console`: local/browser/operator UX for individuals and teams
- `Gunmetal Extensions`: provider/runtime/plugin system

In that structure:
- the console is a surface
- the extension system is infrastructure
- the toll-booth layer is the differentiator

**What to keep**
- The middleman position
- The provider adapter abstraction
- OpenAI-compatible front door
- Shared state across CLI/browser/TUI
- Local-first bias as a wedge

**What to reconsider or cut**
- Treating provider support as hardcoded product scope instead of extension capability
- Duplicated UX surfaces that do the same job
- Product language centered on “bring your subscriptions” only
- Any feature that is just CRUD around profiles/keys without improving control

**A sharper product thesis**
Something like:

`Gunmetal is the control plane for inference traffic. It sits between your apps and model providers, giving you one programmable checkpoint for access, routing, policy, and usage intelligence.`

That is much more expandable than the current framing.

**Strategic risk**
If you lean too hard into “consumer API console,” you build a clever utility.
If you lean too hard into “extension store,” you build a developer library.
If you lean into “control plane,” you can contain both.

**Questions that matter next**
1. Is Gunmetal primarily for individuals, app developers, or teams/orgs?
2. Is local-first a core principle, or just the best initial distribution wedge?
3. What is the one action users should love Gunmetal for in week 1: connect providers, route traffic, or understand/control spend?
4. Do you want Gunmetal to be just a compatibility layer, or an opinionated policy engine?

My current opinion: the right center is not “API keys for subscriptions.” It is “control over inference traffic.” The former is the wedge. The latter is the company.

If you want, next I can turn this into:
1. a one-line positioning statement,
2. a product architecture from first principles,
3. a brutal list of what to delete from the current mental model.

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

# Rules
- Be extremely concise.
- Verify work before reporting back when possible.
- Only stop early when truly blocked.
- If the project already has `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/implement.md`, and `docs/documentation.md`, read them before substantial work and keep them current.
- If the user explicitly asks to create durable memory for a project, create or refresh those files in the project's `docs/` folder before coding, then use them as the source of truth for the rest of the task.

See local guidance when relevant:
- [writing.md](~/.codex/docs/writing.md): how to write replies, plans, and questions.
- [testing.md](~/.codex/docs/testing.md): how to approach tests and edge cases.
- [tooling.md](~/.codex/docs/tooling.md): preferred tools for package management and browser checks.
- [version-control.md](~/.codex/docs/version-control.md): branching, pull requests, commits, and ignore-file habits.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.message:
Yes. I checked [`pi-mono/packages/ai`](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/tree/main/packages/ai) directly.

What it looks like to me:
- It is a unified LLM SDK/library, not a control-plane product.
- Its center is developer integration: provider/model abstraction, streaming, tool-calling, context handoff, token/cost tracking, OAuth-capable providers.
- It already packages providers as a modular dev-facing surface.

So your “extension store” instinct is directionally close, but the comparison also sharpens the difference:
- `pi-ai` is primarily an app-dev SDK.
- `Gunmetal` can be the runtime checkpoint/control plane those SDKs or apps talk through.

That is why I said the extension system is probably infrastructure, not the core product. If you want, next I can do a precise side-by-side:
- `Gunmetal vs pi-ai`
- what to copy
- what to avoid
- what uniquely belongs to Gunmetal.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

vision: 

gunmetal: a middleman for your inference usage. 
- utilize your ai subscriptions as inference via api routing. 

just a first-draft of our core vision/tagline of gunmetal that i could narrow down to. 


now, in order to achieve the core goal, the architecture is as:
upstream provider -> gunmetal (powered by extention store) -> API key
here extention store is nothing but the extentions based modular design that we implemented for providers. but more tightly packaged. 

the above is our core product, thats the main service we provide to our users, in this case, consumers. 

now, being able to sit between the upstream provider and api key (inference usage), it gives us a ability to build products/services on top of this, just as a toll. but this is not a snadalone different product. its just a benefit or advantage of using gunmetal. it comes with it. 

essentially, this is just a single product. but here comes another thing. i want to give the extention store tech architecture as sdandalone to other devs who might want to integrate this in their own software, so that they can also provide multi-provider support. lets say it extention store sdk. now, the thing is, gunmetal also uses this exact sdk for its architecture. there is nothing different. we are just giving it out as a sdk so others can also implement it in their own apps.

payload.message:
vision: 

gunmetal: a middleman for your inference usage. 
- utilize your ai subscriptions as inference via api routing. 

just a first-draft of our core vision/tagline of gunmetal that i could narrow down to. 


now, in order to achieve the core goal, the architecture is as:
upstream provider -> gunmetal (powered by extention store) -> API key
here extention store is nothing but the extentions based modular design that we implemented for providers. but more tightly packaged. 

the above is our core product, thats the main service we provide to our users, in this case, consumers. 

now, being able to sit between the upstream provider and api key (inference usage), it gives us a ability to build products/services on top of this, just as a toll. but this is not a snadalone different product. its just a benefit or advantage of using gunmetal. it comes with it. 

essentially, this is just a single product. but here comes another thing. i want to give the extention store tech architecture as sdandalone to other devs who might want to integrate this in their own software, so that they can also provide multi-provider support. lets say it extention store sdk. now, the thing is, gunmetal also uses this exact sdk for its architecture. there is nothing different. we are just giving it out as a sdk so others can also implement it in their own apps.

This is much cleaner.

The main correction: your architecture should be described from the caller outward, not from upstream inward.

Better:

`app / user tool -> Gunmetal API key -> Gunmetal -> provider extension -> upstream provider`

That matches the actual value:
- the app authenticates to Gunmetal
- Gunmetal decides how to handle the request
- the provider extension speaks upstream
- the upstream provider returns inference

So the **core product** is not “API key routing.” It is:

`Gunmetal is the inference middle layer that turns subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API surface.`

That is strong.

**What I think is right**
- This is one product.
- “Toll booth” is not a separate product. It is the structural advantage of sitting in the path.
- The extension system is the enabling architecture.
- The SDK can be exposed separately, but it should be framed as the same engine Gunmetal uses.

**Important distinction**
I would avoid the term `extension store` unless you literally mean a marketplace/distribution surface.

Right now what you have is closer to:
- `provider runtime`
- `provider SDK`
- `adapter SDK`
- `extension system`

“Store” implies discovery, publishing, versioning, install flows, trust, maybe third-party authors. You may want that later, but it is not the first abstraction.

**Cleaner model**
1. `Gunmetal`
   The product users run to route inference through one control point.

2. `Gunmetal Extensions`
   Provider modules that teach Gunmetal how to talk to upstreams.

3. `Gunmetal SDK`
   The standalone package other developers can use in their own apps to get the same provider-extension system.

That is coherent because:
- Gunmetal uses the SDK itself
- extensions target the same interface
- external devs can embed the same layer without adopting the whole Gunmetal product

**Refined thesis**
Try this:

- `Gunmetal is the middleman for inference usage.`
- `Use your AI subscriptions and provider accounts as one API surface.`
- `Gunmetal sits between your apps and upstream providers, routing inference through a unified control layer.`

Or sharper:

- `Gunmetal turns AI subscriptions into programmable inference.`
- `One control layer between your apps and every model provider.`
- `Bring your subscriptions. Expose them as one API. Control everything in the middle.`

**What uniquely belongs to Gunmetal**
Not just “multi-provider support.” That is table stakes and SDK territory.

Gunmetal’s unique value is:
- turning non-API-native access patterns into usable inference endpoints
- unified auth and provider abstraction
- one local or hosted checkpoint for control
- observability and policy because traffic passes through it

The SDK alone cannot own that. Gunmetal can.

**What I would be careful about**
- If you lead with “SDK,” you drift into infra-library land.
- If you lead with “consumer API console,” you undersell the platform.
- If you lead with “stats,” it sounds shallow.

Lead with:
- unified inference access
- control point
- subscriptions/accounts to API
- extensible provider system underneath

**My recommended framing**
Product:
- `Gunmetal = inference control middle layer`

Architecture:
- `Gunmetal Core`
- `Provider SDK`
- `Provider Extensions`

Built-in advantages:
- routing
- metering
- limits
- attribution
- logging
- future policy/caching/fallback

**One strong wording change**
Instead of:

- `upstream provider -> gunmetal (powered by extention store) -> API key`

Use:

- `client app -> Gunmetal key -> Gunmetal -> provider extension -> upstream provider`

That is materially clearer.

The next hard question is:
Do you want Gunmetal to be primarily `local-first for individuals`, or `deployable control layer for teams`?

That choice will change the whole refactor.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
# Rules
- Be extremely concise.
- Verify work before reporting back when possible.
- Only stop early when truly blocked.
- If the project already has `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/implement.md`, and `docs/documentation.md`, read them before substantial work and keep them current.
- If the user explicitly asks to create durable memory for a project, create or refresh those files in the project's `docs/` folder before coding, then use them as the source of truth for the rest of the task.

See local guidance when relevant:
- [writing.md](~/.codex/docs/writing.md): how to write replies, plans, and questions.
- [testing.md](~/.codex/docs/testing.md): how to approach tests and edge cases.
- [tooling.md](~/.codex/docs/tooling.md): preferred tools for package management and browser checks.
- [version-control.md](~/.codex/docs/version-control.md): branching, pull requests, commits, and ignore-file habits.
- [execution.md](~/.codex/docs/execution.md): how to define done, verify work, and retry before returning.
- [durable-memory.md](~/.codex/docs/durable-memory.md): when to create durable project memory and how to use it.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.

- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
great thesis. now you understand my vision. 

i didnt understand your question.

payload.message:
great thesis. now you understand my vision. 

i didnt understand your question.

The question is about the **primary user and deployment model**.

Two different products can share the same thesis:

**Option A: local-first individual tool**
- A person installs Gunmetal on their machine.
- They connect their own subscriptions/accounts.
- Their apps point to `localhost`.
- The main value is: “use my AI access anywhere.”

**Option B: deployable team control layer**
- A team/org runs Gunmetal as shared infrastructure.
- Multiple users/apps route through it.
- The main value is: governance, usage attribution, budgets, routing, policy.

Both fit your vision. But they force different priorities.

If you choose **A first**:
- local setup matters most
- browser/CLI UX matters most
- provider auth and key creation are the wedge
- “toll booth” features are lighter at first

If you choose **B first**:
- multi-tenant auth matters
- org/user/project attribution matters
- hosted/shared deployment matters
- policy and observability become core immediately

What I’m asking is:

**When Gunmetal is truly good, who should love it first?**
- solo users / prosumers
- app developers embedding it
- teams / orgs

You can eventually support all three. But one has to be the wedge.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: Option A all the way. users and devs should love it.

payload.message: Option A all the way. users and devs should love it.

Good. Then the product gets much simpler.

Primary wedge:
- `Gunmetal is a local-first inference control layer for individuals.`

Secondary audience:
- devs love it because it is extensible and embeddable
- but the product should be designed around the operator who installs it for themselves

That implies a few hard decisions.

**What Gunmetal is**
- a personal inference control center
- a local middle layer between your apps and provider accounts
- a way to turn subscriptions and provider access into one usable API surface

**What it is not, for now**
- not a team platform
- not an enterprise governance product
- not a marketplace company
- not a generic SDK-first library

**What devs get**
- the same extension system Gunmetal uses internally
- the ability to embed provider support in their own software
- but this should not distort the main product UX

So the hierarchy should be:

1. `Gunmetal App/Product`
   For individuals using AI access across tools.

2. `Gunmetal Extension Runtime`
   Internal engine that powers providers.

3. `Gunmetal SDK`
   Externalized version of that runtime for developers.

That keeps the center of gravity in the right place.

**Product consequence**
The first question for every feature becomes:
- does this help a person connect, route, inspect, or control their own inference usage locally?

If not, it is probably distraction.

**This also clarifies the toll-booth layer**
For Option A, the built-in benefits should be personal-control features:
- usage stats
- request history
- model/provider visibility
- routing rules
- maybe budgets/limits
- maybe prompt/log privacy controls
- maybe caching/fallback later

Not org analytics.

**What I would cut from the mental model**
- “platform for everyone”
- “store” as a primary concept
- any early obsession with third-party extension publishing
- team/multi-tenant abstractions

**A sharper product statement**
Try one of these:

- `Gunmetal is your local inference control center.`
- `Use your AI subscriptions anywhere through one local API.`
- `Gunmetal sits between your apps and provider accounts so you can route, control, and inspect inference locally.`

My preferred version:

`Gunmetal is a local-first inference control center that turns your AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.`

That is precise and expandable.

**What this means for the refactor**
You should probably reorganize around 3 layers:

- `core runtime`
  request pipeline, keys, routing, usage tracking, extension host

- `provider extensions`
  codex, copilot, openrouter, openai, etc.

- `operator surfaces`
  local API, browser UI, CLI/TUI

That is cleaner than thinking in terms of the current repo’s feature list.

If you want, next I can do one of these:
1. define the product from first principles in 1 page,
2. design the ideal architecture for this new framing,
3. identify what in the current repo should be deleted, kept, or rewritten.

TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.
