<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: okay, but i dont understand why do we have extention runtime and sdk? cant just this be one thing, like gunmetal app uses gunmetal sdk for implementing multi providers.

payload.message: okay, but i dont understand why do we have extention runtime and sdk? cant just this be one thing, like gunmetal app uses gunmetal sdk for implementing multi providers.

Yes. It can be one thing.

What I was separating was conceptual, not necessarily repo/package count.

The simplest model is:

- `Gunmetal SDK` = the provider-extension system
- `Gunmetal App` uses `Gunmetal SDK`
- external developers can also use `Gunmetal SDK`

That is cleaner.

So internally:

- the app is the product
- the SDK is the engine the app is built on
- provider extensions plug into the SDK

You do **not** need both “extension runtime” and “SDK” as separate branded things unless they become meaningfully different later.

A better structure is:

1. `Gunmetal App`
   The local product users run.

2. `Gunmetal SDK`
   The modular provider framework used by the app and available to other developers.

3. `Gunmetal Extensions`
   Provider modules built against the SDK.

That’s enough.

Why I separated them earlier:
- sometimes the internal runtime evolves differently from the public SDK
- public SDKs need stability guarantees, docs, versioning, narrower interfaces
- internal app code often wants more freedom

But early on, that distinction is unnecessary overhead. Keep them the same thing until reality forces a split.

So yes: your framing is better for now.

**Recommended sentence**
`Gunmetal App uses Gunmetal SDK to support multiple providers through extensions.`

That’s clean.

The only thing I would still avoid is the word `store` unless you truly plan:
- installable third-party extensions
- versioned registry/discovery
- trust/publishing model

If not, call it:
- SDK
- extensions
- adapters
- provider modules

Not store.

If you want, I can now turn this into a concrete product/architecture diagram in plain English.

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.

content.text: i stiill dont understand the diff bw gunmetal entiontions and sdk.

payload.message: i stiill dont understand the diff bw gunmetal entiontions and sdk.

The difference is simple:

- **SDK** = the rules and interfaces
- **Extensions** = the actual provider implementations built using those rules

Analogy:

- `React` is the framework/API
- your components are things built with it

Here:

- `Gunmetal SDK` defines:
  - how a provider plugs in
  - what methods it must implement
  - auth model
  - model listing
  - inference request handling
  - streaming contract
  - metadata/usage contract

- `Gunmetal Extensions` are concrete modules:
  - `codex`
  - `copilot`
  - `openrouter`
  - `openai`

So:

- the **SDK** is the plugin system
- the **extensions** are the plugins

Your app uses both:
- it depends on the SDK
- it loads/extensions built on top of it

In one line:

`SDK = the abstraction`
`Extensions = the implementations`

If even that feels too split, then for now you can think of it as:

- `Gunmetal SDK`
  - includes the extension interface
  - includes bundled first-party provider extensions

Then externally you can say:
- “Gunmetal SDK supports providers through extensions.”

That avoids overcomplicating it.

If you want, I can give you a very concrete example with fake TypeScript/Rust interfaces so it becomes obvious.

- [version-control.md](~/.codex/docs/version-control.md): branching, pull requests, commits, and ignore-file habits.
- [execution.md](~/.codex/docs/execution.md): how to define done, verify work, and retry before returning.
- [durable-memory.md](~/.codex/docs/durable-memory.md): when to create durable project memory and how to use it.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.message:
now got it. good. 

any more things to brainstorm or discuss ?

Yes. The important part now is to force clarity on a few product decisions before touching architecture again.

**Questions to settle**
- What is the single-sentence promise?
  - Example: `Use your AI subscriptions anywhere through one local API.`
- What is the primary object in the system?
  - provider account, route, extension, or key?
- What is the one golden path?
  - install -> connect provider -> create Gunmetal key -> paste into app -> make first request
- What is the minimum “toll booth” value on day 1?
  - request history, usage stats, routing visibility, or policy controls
- What should be first-class in the product model?
  - provider accounts, extensions, keys, models, requests
- What should be hidden?
  - implementation details, provider auth weirdness, extension mechanics
- Is Gunmetal always local-only for the foreseeable future?
  - if yes, that should shape everything
- Is the OpenAI-compatible API the core contract, or just the initial compatibility surface?
- Do you want the SDK to be public immediately, or only after the app architecture stabilizes?

**My opinionated answers**
- Promise: `Use your AI subscriptions and provider accounts through one local API.`
- Primary object: `provider account`
- Golden path: `connect account -> sync models -> mint key -> copy into app -> send request`
- Minimum toll-booth value: `request history + usage accounting + provider/model visibility`
- First-class objects:
  - accounts
  - extensions
  - models
  - keys
  - requests
- Hidden:
  - low-level provider quirks
  - adapter plumbing
- Local-first: yes
- Core contract: OpenAI-compatible API first
- SDK: public later, once the internal interfaces stop moving

**One more thing to resolve**
You need to decide whether Gunmetal is fundamentally about:
- `accounts -> inference`
or
- `requests -> control`

Both are valid, but one should lead the product.

My take:
- the user enters through `accounts -> inference`
- the moat is `requests -> control`

So the UX should sell the first and quietly build toward the second.

If you want, I can next give you a tight artifact:
1. product thesis
2. canonical nouns
3. golden path
4. MVP feature set
5. what to delete from the current app conceptually

That would probably be the right next brainstorming step.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

Good. This narrows things a lot.

A few of your answers are still underspecified, so I’ll tighten them.

**What I now think the product is**
`Gunmetal lets you use your AI subscriptions and provider accounts as API-backed inference through one local middle layer.`

That is your wedge.

**Where I’d sharpen your wording**
“use subscriptions as API key” is slightly imprecise. Better:

- `Turn your AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one local API for inference.`
- `Gunmetal is the middleman inference layer between your apps and AI providers.`

That says the same thing more cleanly.

**Answering the unclear items**
For `2. primary object`, I think it should be:
- `provider account`

Why:
- users think “I connected Codex / Copilot / OpenRouter”
- not “I created an extension” or “I configured a route”
- everything else hangs off that:
  - extensions enable accounts
  - accounts expose models
  - keys authorize access
  - requests use keys against models

For `5. first-class model objects`, I would define:
- provider accounts
- models
- Gunmetal keys
- requests
- usage stats

Maybe extensions too, but only if you want them user-visible.

**The big implication of your answer to 8**
This is important: Gunmetal should not think of itself as only an OpenAI-compatible proxy.

It should think:
- unified local inference layer
- with compatibility surfaces
- OpenAI first
- others later

That is strategically much better.

So:
- canonical internal request model
- compatibility adapters at the edge
- provider extensions on the outbound side

That is the right shape.

**What this suggests architecturally**
Not code yet, just product architecture:

- `Inbound compatibility layer`
  - OpenAI-compatible first
  - later Anthropic/Gemini/etc.

- `Gunmetal core`
  - auth
  - request normalization
  - routing
  - usage tracking
  - request history
  - stats
  - policy hooks later

- `Gunmetal SDK`
  - provider extension contract
  - auth/session model
  - model sync
  - inference execution
  - usage/result normalization

- `Provider extensions`
  - codex
  - copilot
  - openrouter
  - openai
  - later others

That’s much cleaner than the current mental model.

**What you still need to decide**
Two things are still fuzzy:

1. Are extensions visible to users?
Meaning:
- does a user “install an extension”
- or do they just “connect a provider”

For normal users, I strongly recommend:
- users connect providers
- extensions remain mostly hidden

2. What is the first built-in toll-booth benefit?
Pick one cluster, not all:
- request history
- token/cost/usage stats
- model/provider performance visibility
- budgets and limits

My recommendation:
- request history
- token/usage stats
- provider/model visibility

Not budgets yet.

**What I’d challenge directly**
“everything which makes ux for a normal user bad” is correct but too vague. Be stricter:

Delete or hide:
- provider jargon where unnecessary
- extension/store language in end-user UX
- too many surfaces that duplicate each other
- any setup path that does not end in a working copied API key quickly
- raw implementation concepts exposed before first success

**The likely product narrative**
For users:
- connect your accounts
- get one Gunmetal key
- use it anywhere

For the architecture:
- Gunmetal app is powered by an internal SDK
- that SDK may later be published
- but the app is the product, not the SDK

That is coherent.

**What I think we should do next**
We now have enough to define the product properly. The next useful brainstorming step is to lock:

1. canonical nouns
2. user journey
3. MVP boundaries
4. what the current repo should conceptually keep vs drop

If you want, I can do that next in a tight format:
- `Core Thesis`
- `Canonical Objects`
- `Golden Path`
- `MVP`
- `Not Now`
- `Keep / Delete / Rewrite`

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
# Rules
- Be extremely concise.
- Verify work before reporting back when possible.
- Only stop early when truly blocked.
- If the project already has `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/implement.md`, and `docs/documentation.md`, read them before substantial work and keep them current.
- If the user explicitly asks to create durable memory for a project, create or refresh those files in the project's `docs/` folder before coding, then use them as the source of truth for the rest of the task.

See local guidance when relevant:
- [writing.md](~/.codex/docs/writing.md): how to write replies, plans, and questions.
- [testing.md](~/.codex/docs/testing.md): how to approach tests and edge cases.
- [tooling.md](~/.codex/docs/tooling.md): preferred tools for package management and browser checks.
- [version-control.md](~/.codex/docs/version-control.md): branching, pull requests, commits, and ignore-file habits.
- [execution.md](~/.codex/docs/execution.md): how to define done, verify work, and retry before returning.
- [durable-memory.md](~/.codex/docs/durable-memory.md): when to create durable project memory and how to use it.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.message:
1. connect a provider. for us, providers = extentions. keep things extremly simple in terms of ux for users.
2. request history (kind of like logs) and token stats (in/out/etc). 

anything else?

Yes. A few things still matter.

**Strong decisions already made**
- User-facing noun: `provider`
- Internal implementation noun: `extension`
- Core UX: connect provider -> get Gunmetal key -> use anywhere
- Built-in toll-booth value: request history + token stats

That is a solid base.

**My recommendation**
Do not expose `extension` in the normal user UX.
Use:
- `providers` for users
- `extensions` in code/docs/SDK

That keeps the product simple.

**Things still worth deciding**
- Should one Gunmetal key access all connected providers by default, or should keys be provider-scoped by default?
- Should model IDs stay explicit as `provider/model`?
  - I think yes.
- Is Gunmetal always “bring your own provider account,” or will Gunmetal ever sell hosted credits?
  - For now, I’d keep it strictly BYO account.
- What is the first success moment?
  - I’d define it as: first successful inference request from a third-party app, not just provider connection.
- What is the default surface?
  - Browser UI should likely be primary. CLI/TUI can stay, but they should not compete.

**Product principles I’d lock now**
- Local-first
- BYO provider account
- Explicit provider/model IDs
- One fast golden path
- Provider quirks hidden where possible
- Observability included by default
- SDK powers the app, but the app remains the product

**What not to do**
- Don’t expose store/extension concepts too early
- Don’t add team/platform abstractions
- Don’t broaden compatibility surfaces too early
- Don’t let setup end before the user has a real working request

**One subtle but important point**
Your real product object flow is probably:

`Provider -> Models -> Gunmetal Key -> Requests -> Usage`

That gives you the product information architecture.

If you want, the next useful step is for me to turn everything we’ve agreed into a tight first-principles spec:
- product thesis
- canonical nouns
- golden path
- MVP scope
- keep/delete/rewrite from current Gunmetal

- If the user explicitly asks to create durable memory for a project, create or refresh those files in the project's `docs/` folder before coding, then use them as the source of truth for the rest of the task.

See local guidance when relevant:
- [writing.md](~/.codex/docs/writing.md): how to write replies, plans, and questions.
- [testing.md](~/.codex/docs/testing.md): how to approach tests and edge cases.
- [tooling.md](~/.codex/docs/tooling.md): preferred tools for package management and browser checks.
- [version-control.md](~/.codex/docs/version-control.md): branching, pull requests, commits, and ignore-file habits.
- [execution.md](~/.codex/docs/execution.md): how to define done, verify work, and retry before returning.
- [durable-memory.md](~/.codex/docs/durable-memory.md): when to create durable project memory and how to use it.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

  - `git log --oneline --decorate --graph --all -n 80`
  - `git log --reverse --oneline --no-decorate`
  - `git rev-list --count --all`
  - `git tag --sort=creatordate`
- Workspace inspection:
  - `find apps packages -maxdepth 2 -type f | sort`
  - targeted `sed -n` reads of `README.md`, workspace manifests, and key source files
- Durable-memory verification:
  - `test -f docs/prompt.md && test -f docs/plans.md && test -f docs/implement.md && test -f docs/documentation.md`
  - direct review of the new docs

## Completed Work
- Captured the current repo organization:
  - `apps/cli`: Rust entrypoint that boots the daemon for default/TUI usage or dispatches CLI commands.
  - `apps/web`: Next.js hosted front door with install/docs/changelog/web-ui pages.
  - `packages/cli`: command parsing and operational flows such as setup, web, start, status, profiles, auth, models, keys, and logs.
  - `packages/daemon`: local HTTP service exposing `/health`, browser control-plane endpoints under `/app/api/*`, and `/v1/models`, `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/responses`.
  - `packages/providers`: provider adapters and registry/hub logic.
  - `packages/storage`: local app-path resolution and SQLite-backed persistence.
  - `packages/core`: shared domain types and API contracts.
  - `packages/tui`: terminal dashboard/operator surface.
  - `packages/npm`: npm distribution wrapper that installs the native binary.
- Captured the visible work-history arc from git:
  - foundation and bootstrap
  - provider rollout for codex, copilot, openrouter, zen, and openai
  - responses API support
  - first-run and TUI hardening
  - monorepo restructure
  - npm packaging and release pipeline
  - hosted site plus local browser UI
  - several release/CI/acceptance stabilization cycles
  - latest visible release tag: `v0.1.8`

## Validation Results
- The repo was clean before this task except for branch state.
- The new durable-memory files now exist in `docs/`.
- The docs match the inspected workspace and history and do not claim unverified roadmap work.

## Decisions
- Use the durable-memory set for future project work in this repo.
- Keep the memory anchored to the current task and actual repo state instead of turning it into generic project notes.
- Treat git history as the source for fine-grained progress details when later work needs subsystem-level context.

## Next Steps
- Start the next requested task using `docs/prompt.md` and `docs/plans.md` as the baseline.
- Refresh the durable-memory files at the start of the next substantive task so the active scope is explicit.

## Follow-Ups
- If the next task targets `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` before making web changes.
- If the next task depends on a specific regression or release, inspect the relevant commits directly instead of relying only on this summary.

payload.message: I’ve confirmed the current boundary: `packages/providers` is already doing two jobs at once, the SDK-like abstraction layer and the built-in provider implementations. I’m splitting those roles now so the app depends on an explicit internal SDK crate while the current providers package becomes first-party extensions on top of it.

+- Risk: over-designing the SDK too early.

-- Core storage: local files under `~/.gunmetal`, with SQLite state, runtime files, helper directory, and logs.
-- Provider model: explicit adapters for `codex`, `copilot`, `openrouter`, `zen`, and `openai`, with additional enum slots for `azure`, `nvidia`, and custom providers.

# Prompt

## Task
- Refactor `gunmetal` from the newly agreed first-principles product direction. First update durable memory to reflect the new thesis. Then reshape the internals so Gunmetal App is explicitly powered by an internal SDK layer with provider extensions on top. After that internal SDK extraction is complete, later work can focus on perfecting the user-facing CLI, TUI, and web UX.

## Core Goal
- Make the app architecture match the product thesis: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer, and its provider system is an internal SDK that also powers first-party provider extensions.

## Goals
- Update durable memory for the new product positioning and implementation order.
- Extract the current provider abstraction/runtime into an explicit internal SDK package.
- Keep Gunmetal App using that SDK internally; do not publish the SDK yet.
- Leave provider implementations as first-party extensions built on the SDK.
- Preserve current behavior as much as possible while changing internal ownership boundaries.

## Non-Goals
- Do not optimize the end-user UX in this pass beyond what the internal refactor requires.
- Do not publish or document the SDK as a public package yet.
- Do not broaden compatibility surfaces beyond the current product behavior in this pass.
- Do not add team or multi-tenant product concerns.

## Constraints
- Be concise.
- Verify the work before reporting back when possible.
- Use the durable-memory files as the source of truth for subsequent work in this repo.
- Keep the product framing simple for users: user-facing noun is `provider`, internal noun is `extension`.
- The product is local-first and individual-first.
- Gunmetal remains a single product, not multiple separate apps/repos.

## Deliverables
- Updated durable-memory docs for the new thesis
- Internal SDK package and dependency wiring
- App crates updated to depend on the SDK-backed provider system
- Verification notes for the refactor

## Process Requirements
- Update durable memory before broad implementation.
- Work in two phases:
  - phase 1: durable-memory refresh
  - phase 2: internal SDK extraction and app rewiring
- Keep the docs current as architecture decisions land.

## Done When
- Durable memory reflects the new product thesis and refactor scope.
- The app is powered by an explicit internal SDK layer.
- First-party providers are implemented as extensions on that SDK.
- The repo still builds/tests for the touched areas.

# Plans

This document is the execution plan and ongoing notes for the current task. Keep it current as the work moves.

## Verification Checklist
- `sed -n '1,220p' docs/prompt.md`
- `sed -n '1,260p' docs/plans.md`
- `sed -n '1,260p' docs/documentation.md`
- targeted cargo tests for the SDK/providers/app boundaries
- `cargo test --workspace` if the full workspace remains green within reasonable time
- Last verified: 2026-04-12

## Milestones
- `[in progress]` Milestone 1: refresh durable memory for the new product direction
  - Scope: rewrite product framing, architecture notes, and refactor order
  - Key areas: `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/documentation.md`
  - Acceptance criteria: docs reflect the agreed thesis that Gunmetal App is the product and an internal SDK powers first-party provider extensions
  - Verification steps: direct review of the updated docs
- `[pending]` Milestone 2: extract internal SDK boundary
  - Scope: create a dedicated internal package for the provider/extension abstraction, registry, hub, stream helpers, and model metadata enrichment
  - Key areas: new SDK package plus Cargo workspace wiring
  - Acceptance criteria: the provider abstraction no longer lives only inside `packages/providers`
  - Verification steps: targeted cargo tests for the new SDK package
- `[pending]` Milestone 3: rewire first-party providers and app crates
  - Scope: move first-party provider implementations onto the SDK and update app crates to depend on the new boundary
  - Key areas: `packages/providers`, `packages/cli`, `packages/daemon`, `packages/tui`
  - Acceptance criteria: app crates compile against the SDK-backed provider system with no behavior regressions in touched tests
  - Verification steps: targeted cargo tests, then broader workspace tests if feasible

## Acceptance Checks
- Durable memory states the final product decisions from the brainstorming session.
- An explicit SDK crate exists and owns the provider-extension abstraction layer.
- `packages/providers` acts as first-party extensions on top of the SDK rather than owning the whole abstraction itself.
- App crates consume the SDK-backed provider system successfully.

## Validation
- `git status --short --branch`
- targeted `sed -n` reads across docs and provider/runtime source files
- targeted cargo tests for SDK/providers/app crates
- `cargo test --workspace`

## Decisions
- Product thesis: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer that turns AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.
- User-facing noun is `provider`; internal implementation noun is `extension`.
- The app is the product. The SDK is internal first and powers the app.
- Request history and token stats are the first built-in toll-booth benefits to preserve and strengthen later.
- The current pass is architecture-first, not UX-polish-first.

## Implementation Notes
- Current branch for this work: `refactor/internal-sdk`.
- The existing `packages/providers` crate currently mixes the SDK-like abstraction layer with first-party provider implementations.
- The desired boundary is:
  - `Gunmetal App` uses `Gunmetal SDK`
  - `Gunmetal SDK` defines the provider-extension contract and runtime
  - `packages/providers` supplies first-party provider extensions built on the SDK
- Current app crates directly import `gunmetal-providers`; those imports will need to shift or be re-exported cleanly.

## Risks
- Risk: extracting the SDK boundary causes broad compile churn across CLI, daemon, TUI, and tests.
  - Mitigation: keep names stable where possible and change ownership first before renaming concepts.
- Risk: refactor accidentally changes runtime behavior.
  - Mitigation: preserve the current hub/registry semantics and keep targeted tests close to moved code.
- Risk: over-designing the SDK too early.
  - Mitigation: keep it internal and shaped by current app needs, not hypothetical external users.

## Architecture
- Product: local-first inference middle layer for individuals.
- Canonical flow: `app/tool -> Gunmetal key -> Gunmetal -> provider extension -> upstream provider`.
- Core product nouns:
  - provider accounts
  - models
  - Gunmetal keys
  - requests
  - usage stats
- Internal architecture target:
  - inbound compatibility layer
  - Gunmetal core request/control layer
  - Gunmetal SDK for provider extensions
  - first-party provider extensions

  - Acceptance criteria: docs reflect the agreed thesis that Gunmetal App is the product and an internal SDK powers first-party provider extensions
  - Verification steps: direct review of the updated docs
- `[pending]` Milestone 2: extract internal SDK boundary
  - Scope: create a dedicated internal package for the provider/extension abstraction, registry, hub, stream helpers, and model metadata enrichment
  - Key areas: new SDK package plus Cargo workspace wiring
  - Acceptance criteria: the provider abstraction no longer lives only inside `packages/providers`
  - Verification steps: targeted cargo tests for the new SDK package
- `[pending]` Milestone 3: rewire first-party providers and app crates
  - Scope: move first-party provider implementations onto the SDK and update app crates to depend on the new boundary
  - Key areas: `packages/providers`, `packages/cli`, `packages/daemon`, `packages/tui`
  - Acceptance criteria: app crates compile against the SDK-backed provider system with no behavior regressions in touched tests
  - Verification steps: targeted cargo tests, then broader workspace tests if feasible

## Acceptance Checks
- Durable memory states the final product decisions from the brainstorming session.
- An explicit SDK crate exists and owns the provider-extension abstraction layer.
- `packages/providers` acts as first-party extensions on top of the SDK rather than owning the whole abstraction itself.
- App crates consume the SDK-backed provider system successfully.

## Validation
- `git status --short --branch`
- targeted `sed -n` reads across docs and provider/runtime source files
- targeted cargo tests for SDK/providers/app crates
- `cargo test --workspace`

## Decisions
- Product thesis: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer that turns AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.
- User-facing noun is `provider`; internal implementation noun is `extension`.
- The app is the product. The SDK is internal first and powers the app.
- Request history and token stats are the first built-in toll-booth benefits to preserve and strengthen later.
- The current pass is architecture-first, not UX-polish-first.

## Implementation Notes
- Current branch for this work: `refactor/internal-sdk`.
- The existing `packages/providers` crate currently mixes the SDK-like abstraction layer with first-party provider implementations.
- The desired boundary is:
  - `Gunmetal App` uses `Gunmetal SDK`
  - `Gunmetal SDK` defines the provider-extension contract and runtime
  - `packages/providers` supplies first-party provider extensions built on the SDK
- Current app crates directly import `gunmetal-providers`; those imports will need to shift or be re-exported cleanly.

## Risks
- Risk: extracting the SDK boundary causes broad compile churn across CLI, daemon, TUI, and tests.
  - Mitigation: keep names stable where possible and change ownership first before renaming concepts.
- Risk: refactor accidentally changes runtime behavior.
  - Mitigation: preserve the current hub/registry semantics and keep targeted tests close to moved code.
- Risk: over-designing the SDK too early.
  - Mitigation: keep it internal and shaped by current app needs, not hypothetical external users.

## Architecture
- Product: local-first inference middle layer for individuals.
- Canonical flow: `app/tool -> Gunmetal key -> Gunmetal -> provider extension -> upstream provider`.
- Core product nouns:
  - provider accounts
  - models
  - Gunmetal keys
  - requests
  - usage stats
- Internal architecture target:
  - inbound compatibility layer
  - Gunmetal core request/control layer
  - Gunmetal SDK for provider extensions
  - first-party provider extensions

# Documentation

This document is updated continuously as milestones land so it reflects reality.

## What This Work Is
- This pass starts the first-principles refactor. Durable memory is now being updated to reflect the new product thesis, and the next implementation step is to extract an internal SDK boundary so the app is powered by a dedicated provider-extension engine.

## Current Status
- Durable memory exists and is now updated for the new direction.
- Product thesis is now: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer that turns AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.
- User-facing term is `provider`; internal implementation term is `extension`.
- Current branch for this refactor: `refactor/internal-sdk`.

## Status By Milestone
- Milestone 1 in progress: durable-memory refresh for the new thesis.
- Milestone 2 pending: extract internal SDK boundary.
- Milestone 3 pending: rewire first-party providers and app crates onto the SDK.

## Setup And Verification
- Repo state:
  - `git status --short --branch`
  - `git log --oneline --decorate --graph --all -n 80`
  - `git log --reverse --oneline --no-decorate`
  - `git rev-list --count --all`
  - `git tag --sort=creatordate`
- Workspace inspection:
  - `find apps packages -maxdepth 2 -type f | sort`
  - targeted `sed -n` reads of `README.md`, workspace manifests, and key source files
- Durable-memory verification:
  - `test -f docs/prompt.md && test -f docs/plans.md && test -f docs/implement.md && test -f docs/documentation.md`
  - direct review of the new docs

## Completed Work
- Established the new product decisions from brainstorming:
  - Gunmetal is one product, not three.
  - Gunmetal App is the local-first product for individuals.
  - An internal Gunmetal SDK powers the app’s multi-provider support.
  - First-party providers are implemented as extensions on top of that SDK.
  - The first built-in toll-booth benefits are request history and token stats.
- Confirmed the current codebase shape relevant to the refactor:
  - `packages/providers` currently owns both the abstraction layer and the built-in provider implementations.
  - `packages/cli`, `packages/daemon`, and `packages/tui` depend directly on `gunmetal-providers`.

## Validation Results
- The durable-memory files were reviewed and updated to match the new product direction.
- The current provider/runtime boundary has been inspected before starting the code refactor.

## Decisions
- Product promise: use AI subscriptions and provider accounts through one local middle layer for inference.
- Gunmetal’s current OpenAI-compatible surface is the initial compatibility layer, not the permanent only abstraction.
- The SDK stays internal until the app architecture is tightly packed and stable.
- UX should stay extremely simple for normal users: connect providers, mint a key, make requests.

## Next Steps
- Finish the SDK extraction and move the provider abstraction out of `packages/providers`.
- Rewire app crates to depend on the SDK-backed provider system.
- After the internal boundary is solid, shift focus to CLI/TUI/web usability.

## Follow-Ups
- Later work should decide how much of the SDK becomes public API and how much remains internal.
- Future compatibility work should likely happen through inbound compatibility adapters, not by forcing everything into one OpenAI-only surface.

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /Users/dhruv2mars/dev/github/gunmetal/docs/plans.md
@@
 - `sed -n '1,220p' docs/prompt.md`
 - `sed -n '1,260p' docs/plans.md`
 - `sed -n '1,260p' docs/documentation.md`
+- `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
+- `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
+- `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
 - targeted cargo tests for the SDK/providers/app boundaries
 - `cargo test --workspace` if the full workspace remains green within reasonable time
 - Last verified: 2026-04-12
 
 ## Milestones
-- `[in progress]` Milestone 1: refresh durable memory for the new product direction
+- `[done]` Milestone 1: refresh durable memory for the new product direction
   - Scope: rewrite product framing, architecture notes, and refactor order
   - Key areas: `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/documentation.md`
   - Acceptance criteria: docs reflect the agreed thesis that Gunmetal App is the product and an internal SDK powers first-party provider extensions
   - Verification steps: direct review of the updated docs
-- `[pending]` Milestone 2: extract internal SDK boundary
+- `[done]` Milestone 2: extract internal SDK boundary
   - Scope: create a dedicated internal package for the provider/extension abstraction, registry, hub, stream helpers, and model metadata enrichment
   - Key areas: new SDK package plus Cargo workspace wiring
   - Acceptance criteria: the provider abstraction no longer lives only inside `packages/providers`
   - Verification steps: targeted cargo tests for the new SDK package
-- `[pending]` Milestone 3: rewire first-party providers and app crates
+- `[done]` Milestone 3: rewire first-party providers and app crates
   - Scope: move first-party provider implementations onto the SDK and update app crates to depend on the new boundary
   - Key areas: `packages/providers`, `packages/cli`, `packages/daemon`, `packages/tui`
   - Acceptance criteria: app crates compile against the SDK-backed provider system with no behavior regressions in touched tests
   - Verification steps: targeted cargo tests, then broader workspace tests if feasible
@@
 - `git status --short --branch`
 - targeted `sed -n` reads across docs and provider/runtime source files
+- `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
+- `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
+- `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
 - targeted cargo tests for SDK/providers/app crates
 - `cargo test --workspace`
@@
 - Current branch for this work: `refactor/internal-sdk`.
 - The existing `packages/providers` crate currently mixes the SDK-like abstraction layer with first-party provider implementations.
 - The desired boundary is:
   - `Gunmetal App` uses `Gunmetal SDK`
   - `Gunmetal SDK` defines the provider-extension contract and runtime
   - `packages/providers` supplies first-party provider extensions built on the SDK
-- Current app crates directly import `gunmetal-providers`; those imports will need to shift or be re-exported cleanly.
+- Implemented boundary:
+  - new crate: `packages/sdk`
+  - `packages/sdk` now owns `ProviderAdapter`, `ProviderRegistry`, `ProviderHub`, stream helpers, and models.dev enrichment
+  - `packages/providers` now owns first-party provider extensions plus builtin registry wiring
+  - app crates use the SDK boundary directly where runtime types matter and use `packages/providers` for builtin defaults
*** Update File: /Users/dhruv2mars/dev/github/gunmetal/docs/documentation.md
@@
 ## Current Status
 - Durable memory exists and is now updated for the new direction.
 - Product thesis is now: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer that turns AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.
 - User-facing term is `provider`; internal implementation term is `extension`.
 - Current branch for this refactor: `refactor/internal-sdk`.
+- The internal SDK extraction is now implemented and verified.
 
 ## Status By Milestone
-- Milestone 1 in progress: durable-memory refresh for the new thesis.
-- Milestone 2 pending: extract internal SDK boundary.
-- Milestone 3 pending: rewire first-party providers and app crates onto the SDK.
+- Milestone 1 complete: durable-memory refresh for the new thesis.
+- Milestone 2 complete: extracted internal SDK boundary.
+- Milestone 3 complete: rewired first-party providers and app crates onto the SDK-backed layout.
@@
 - Established the new product decisions from brainstorming:
   - Gunmetal is one product, not three.
   - Gunmetal App is the local-first product for individuals.
   - An internal Gunmetal SDK powers the app’s multi-provider support.
   - First-party providers are implemented as extensions on top of that SDK.
   - The first built-in toll-booth benefits are request history and token stats.
-- Confirmed the current codebase shape relevant to the refactor:
-  - `packages/providers` currently owns both the abstraction layer and the built-in provider implementations.
-  - `packages/cli`, `packages/daemon`, and `packages/tui` depend directly on `gunmetal-providers`.
+- Implemented the new internal boundary:
+  - added `packages/sdk` as the internal SDK crate
+  - moved the provider abstraction/runtime layer into `packages/sdk`
+  - kept first-party provider clients and extension implementations in `packages/providers`
+  - added builtin registry and builtin provider-hub wiring in `packages/providers`
+  - rewired CLI, daemon, and TUI to use the SDK-backed provider system
@@
 ## Validation Results
 - The durable-memory files were reviewed and updated to match the new product direction.
 - The current provider/runtime boundary has been inspected before starting the code refactor.
+- `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk` passed.
+- `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers` passed.
+- `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui` passed.
+- `cargo test --workspace` passed.
@@
 ## Next Steps
-- Finish the SDK extraction and move the provider abstraction out of `packages/providers`.
-- Rewire app crates to depend on the SDK-backed provider system.
-- After the internal boundary is solid, shift focus to CLI/TUI/web usability.
+- Use this new boundary as the base for the next phase: make the CLI, TUI, and web/browser UX feel tightly packed and simple for normal users.
+- Decide how much of the current compatibility surface and provider setup flow should be simplified in the product-facing UX.
*** End Patch

payload.message:
The first refactor phase is done.

I updated the durable-memory docs to match the new thesis and implementation order in [docs/prompt.md](/Users/dhruv2mars/dev/github/gunmetal/docs/prompt.md), [docs/plans.md](/Users/dhruv2mars/dev/github/gunmetal/docs/plans.md), and [docs/documentation.md](/Users/dhruv2mars/dev/github/gunmetal/docs/documentation.md). Then I extracted a new internal SDK crate at [packages/sdk/Cargo.toml](/Users/dhruv2mars/dev/github/gunmetal/packages/sdk/Cargo.toml) and [packages/sdk/src/lib.rs](/Users/dhruv2mars/dev/github/gunmetal/packages/sdk/src/lib.rs). That crate now owns the provider-extension abstraction and runtime pieces: `ProviderAdapter`, `ProviderRegistry`, `ProviderHub`, stream helpers, and models.dev enrichment.

`[packages/providers/src/lib.rs](/Users/dhruv2mars/dev/github/gunmetal/packages/providers/src/lib.rs)` is now reduced to what it should be: first-party provider extensions plus builtin registry wiring. CLI, daemon, and TUI were rewired onto that SDK-backed layout, while preserving current behavior.

Verification is green:
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
- `cargo test --workspace`

The repo is ready for the next phase: simplifying and tightening the user-facing CLI, TUI, and web/browser UX on top of this new boundary.
