# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

# Rules
- Be extremely concise.
- Verify work before reporting back when possible.
- Only stop early when truly blocked.
- If the project already has `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/implement.md`, and `docs/documentation.md`, read them before substantial work and keep them current.
- If the user explicitly asks to create durable memory for a project, create or refresh those files in the project's `docs/` folder before coding, then use them as the source of truth for the rest of the task.

See local guidance when relevant:
- [writing.md](~/.codex/docs/writing.md): how to write replies, plans, and questions.
- [testing.md](~/.codex/docs/testing.md): how to approach tests and edge cases.
- [tooling.md](~/.codex/docs/tooling.md): preferred tools for package management and browser checks.
- [version-control.md](~/.codex/docs/version-control.md): branching, pull requests, commits, and ignore-file habits.
- [execution.md](~/.codex/docs/execution.md): how to define done, verify work, and retry before returning.
- [durable-memory.md](~/.codex/docs/durable-memory.md): when to create durable project memory and how to use it.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

# Prompt

## Task
- Refactor `gunmetal` from the newly agreed first-principles product direction. First update durable memory to reflect the new thesis. Then reshape the internals so Gunmetal App is explicitly powered by an internal SDK layer with provider extensions on top. After that internal SDK extraction is complete, later work can focus on perfecting the user-facing CLI, TUI, and web UX.

## Core Goal
- Make the app architecture match the product thesis: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer, and its provider system is an internal SDK that also powers first-party provider extensions.

## Goals
- Update durable memory for the new product positioning and implementation order.
- Extract the current provider abstraction/runtime into an explicit internal SDK package.
- Keep Gunmetal App using that SDK internally; do not publish the SDK yet.
- Leave provider implementations as first-party extensions built on the SDK.
- Preserve current behavior as much as possible while changing internal ownership boundaries.

## Non-Goals
- Do not optimize the end-user UX in this pass beyond what the internal refactor requires.
- Do not publish or document the SDK as a public package yet.
- Do not broaden compatibility surfaces beyond the current product behavior in this pass.
- Do not add team or multi-tenant product concerns.

## Constraints
- Be concise.
- Verify the work before reporting back when possible.
- Use the durable-memory files as the source of truth for subsequent work in this repo.
- Keep the product framing simple for users: user-facing noun is `provider`, internal noun is `extension`.
- The product is local-first and individual-first.
- Gunmetal remains a single product, not multiple separate apps/repos.

## Deliverables
- Updated durable-memory docs for the new thesis
- Internal SDK package and dependency wiring
- App crates updated to depend on the SDK-backed provider system
- Verification notes for the refactor

## Process Requirements
- Update durable memory before broad implementation.
- Work in two phases:
  - phase 1: durable-memory refresh
  - phase 2: internal SDK extraction and app rewiring
- Keep the docs current as architecture decisions land.

## Done When
- Durable memory reflects the new product thesis and refactor scope.
- The app is powered by an explicit internal SDK layer.
- First-party providers are implemented as extensions on that SDK.
- The repo still builds/tests for the touched areas.

# Plans

This document is the execution plan and ongoing notes for the current task. Keep it current as the work moves.

## Verification Checklist
- `sed -n '1,220p' docs/prompt.md`
- `sed -n '1,260p' docs/plans.md`
- `sed -n '1,260p' docs/documentation.md`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
- targeted cargo tests for the SDK/providers/app boundaries
- `cargo test --workspace` if the full workspace remains green within reasonable time
- Last verified: 2026-04-12

## Milestones
- `[done]` Milestone 1: refresh durable memory for the new product direction
  - Scope: rewrite product framing, architecture notes, and refactor order
  - Key areas: `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/documentation.md`
  - Acceptance criteria: docs reflect the agreed thesis that Gunmetal App is the product and an internal SDK powers first-party provider extensions
  - Verification steps: direct review of the updated docs
- `[done]` Milestone 2: extract internal SDK boundary
  - Scope: create a dedicated internal package for the provider/extension abstraction, registry, hub, stream helpers, and model metadata enrichment
  - Key areas: new SDK package plus Cargo workspace wiring
  - Acceptance criteria: the provider abstraction no longer lives only inside `packages/providers`
  - Verification steps: targeted cargo tests for the new SDK package
- `[done]` Milestone 3: rewire first-party providers and app crates
  - Scope: move first-party provider implementations onto the SDK and update app crates to depend on the new boundary
  - Key areas: `packages/providers`, `packages/cli`, `packages/daemon`, `packages/tui`
  - Acceptance criteria: app crates compile against the SDK-backed provider system with no behavior regressions in touched tests
  - Verification steps: targeted cargo tests, then broader workspace tests if feasible

## Acceptance Checks
- Durable memory states the final product decisions from the brainstorming session.
- An explicit SDK crate exists and owns the provider-extension abstraction layer.
- `packages/providers` acts as first-party extensions on top of the SDK rather than owning the whole abstraction itself.
- App crates consume the SDK-backed provider system successfully.

## Validation
- `git status --short --branch`
- targeted `sed -n` reads across docs and provider/runtime source files
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
- targeted cargo tests for SDK/providers/app crates
- `cargo test --workspace`

## Decisions
- Product thesis: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer that turns AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.
- User-facing noun is `provider`; internal implementation noun is `extension`.
- The app is the product. The SDK is internal first and powers the app.
- Request history and token stats are the first built-in toll-booth benefits to preserve and strengthen later.
- The current pass is architecture-first, not UX-polish-first.

## Implementation Notes
- Current branch for this work: `refactor/internal-sdk`.
- The existing `packages/providers` crate currently mixes the SDK-like abstraction layer with first-party provider implementations.
- The desired boundary is:
  - `Gunmetal App` uses `Gunmetal SDK`
  - `Gunmetal SDK` defines the provider-extension contract and runtime
  - `packages/providers` supplies first-party provider extensions built on the SDK
- Implemented boundary:
  - new crate: `packages/sdk`
  - `packages/sdk` now owns `ProviderAdapter`, `ProviderRegistry`, `ProviderHub`, stream helpers, and models.dev enrichment
  - `packages/providers` now owns first-party provider extensions plus builtin registry wiring
  - app crates use the SDK boundary directly where runtime types matter and use `packages/providers` for builtin defaults

## Risks
- Risk: extracting the SDK boundary causes broad compile churn across CLI, daemon, TUI, and tests.
  - Mitigation: keep names stable where possible and change ownership first before renaming concepts.
- Risk: refactor accidentally changes runtime behavior.
  - Mitigation: preserve the current hub/registry semantics and keep targeted tests close to moved code.
- Risk: over-designing the SDK too early.
  - Mitigation: keep it internal and shaped by current app needs, not hypothetical external users.

## Architecture
- Product: local-first inference middle layer for individuals.
- Canonical flow: `app/tool -> Gunmetal key -> Gunmetal -> provider extension -> upstream provider`.
- Core product nouns:
  - provider accounts
  - models
  - Gunmetal keys
  - requests
  - usage stats
- Internal architecture target:
  - inbound compatibility layer
  - Gunmetal core request/control layer
  - Gunmetal SDK for provider extensions
  - first-party provider extensions

# Documentation

This document is updated continuously as milestones land so it reflects reality.

## What This Work Is
- This pass starts the first-principles refactor. Durable memory is now being updated to reflect the new product thesis, and the next implementation step is to extract an internal SDK boundary so the app is powered by a dedicated provider-extension engine.

## Current Status
- Durable memory exists and is now updated for the new direction.
- Product thesis is now: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer that turns AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.
- User-facing term is `provider`; internal implementation term is `extension`.
- Current branch for this refactor: `refactor/internal-sdk`.
- The internal SDK extraction is now implemented and verified.

## Status By Milestone
- Milestone 1 complete: durable-memory refresh for the new thesis.
- Milestone 2 complete: extracted internal SDK boundary.
- Milestone 3 complete: rewired first-party providers and app crates onto the SDK-backed layout.

## Setup And Verification
- Repo state:
  - `git status --short --branch`
  - `git log --oneline --decorate --graph --all -n 80`
  - `git log --reverse --oneline --no-decorate`
  - `git rev-list --count --all`
  - `git tag --sort=creatordate`
- Workspace inspection:
  - `find apps packages -maxdepth 2 -type f | sort`
  - targeted `sed -n` reads of `README.md`, workspace manifests, and key source files
- Durable-memory verification:
  - `test -f docs/prompt.md && test -f docs/plans.md && test -f docs/implement.md && test -f docs/documentation.md`
  - direct review of the new docs

## Completed Work
- Established the new product decisions from brainstorming:
  - Gunmetal is one product, not three.
  - Gunmetal App is the local-first product for individuals.
  - An internal Gunmetal SDK powers the app’s multi-provider support.
  - First-party providers are implemented as extensions on top of that SDK.
  - The first built-in toll-booth benefits are request history and token stats.
- Implemented the new internal boundary:
  - added `packages/sdk` as the internal SDK crate
  - moved the provider abstraction/runtime layer into `packages/sdk`
  - kept first-party provider clients and extension implementations in `packages/providers`
  - added builtin registry and builtin provider-hub wiring in `packages/providers`
  - rewired CLI, daemon, and TUI to use the SDK-backed provider system

## Validation Results
- The durable-memory files were reviewed and updated to match the new product direction.
- The current provider/runtime boundary has been inspected before starting the code refactor.
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk` passed.
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers` passed.
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui` passed.
- `cargo test --workspace` passed.

## Decisions
- Product promise: use AI subscriptions and provider accounts through one local middle layer for inference.
- Gunmetal’s current OpenAI-compatible surface is the initial compatibility layer, not the permanent only abstraction.
- The SDK stays internal until the app architecture is tightly packed and stable.
- UX should stay extremely simple for normal users: connect providers, mint a key, make requests.

## Next Steps
- Use this new boundary as the base for the next phase: make the CLI, TUI, and web/browser UX feel tightly packed and simple for normal users.
- Decide how much of the current compatibility surface and provider setup flow should be simplified in the product-facing UX.

## Follow-Ups
- Later work should decide how much of the SDK becomes public API and how much remains internal.
- Future compatibility work should likely happen through inbound compatibility adapters, not by forcing everything into one OpenAI-only surface.

+# Prompt
+
+## Task
+- Continue the first-principles refactor by restructuring the repository itself around the new architecture. The SDK boundary already exists. Now the repo layout should make that split obvious: Gunmetal App as the product, Gunmetal SDK as the engine, and first-party extensions as the provider implementation layer.
+
+## Core Goal
+- Make the repository structure match the product architecture so future SDK publication and app work both fit naturally.
+
+## Goals
+- Update durable memory for the repo-structure refactor scope.
+- Reorganize directories so the app, SDK, and extensions are clearly separated.
+- Keep crate/package identities stable where practical while changing paths and workspace structure.
+- Preserve behavior and keep the workspace green after the moves.
+
+## Non-Goals
+- Do not redesign the user-facing UX in this pass.
+- Do not publish the SDK yet.
+- Do not change runtime behavior beyond what path/reference updates require.
+- Do not split this into multiple repos.
+
+## Constraints
+- Be concise.
+- Verify the work before reporting back when possible.
+- Use the durable-memory files as the source of truth for subsequent work in this repo.
+- Keep the product framing simple for users: user-facing noun is `provider`, internal noun is `extension`.
+- The product is local-first and individual-first.
+- Gunmetal remains a single product, not multiple separate apps/repos.
+- Favor directory/package moves over semantic renames of crate imports unless needed.
+
+## Deliverables
+- Updated durable-memory docs for the repo-structure pass
+- Reorganized workspace directories and manifest paths
+- Updated docs/build references to the new layout
+- Verification notes for the restructure
+
+## Process Requirements
+- Update durable memory before broad implementation.
+- Work in two phases:
+  - phase 1: durable-memory refresh
+  - phase 2: directory/workspace restructure and reference updates
+- Keep the docs current as architecture decisions land.
+
+## Done When
+- Durable memory reflects the repo-structure decisions.
+- The repo tree clearly separates app packages, SDK, and extensions.
+- Workspace manifests and docs point to the new layout.
+- The repo still builds/tests for the touched areas.

+- Risk: the new tree optimizes for theory over current use.
+  - Mitigation: keep the layout minimal and directly tied to the existing app/sdk/extensions split.

+- Canonical flow: `app/tool -> Gunmetal key -> Gunmetal -> provider extension -> upstream provider`.

+  - requests
+  - usage stats
+- Internal architecture target:
+  - inbound compatibility layer
+  - Gunmetal core request/control layer
+  - Gunmetal SDK for provider extensions
+  - first-party provider extensions

# Documentation

This document is updated continuously as milestones land so it reflects reality.

## What This Work Is
- This pass continues the first-principles refactor by aligning the repository layout with the new architecture. The SDK boundary already exists. Now the tree itself should clearly show Gunmetal App, Gunmetal SDK, and first-party extensions as separate concerns.

## Current Status
- Durable memory exists and is updated for the repo-structure pass.
- Product thesis: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer that turns AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.
- User-facing term is `provider`; internal implementation term is `extension`.
- Current branch for this refactor: `refactor/internal-sdk`.
- The internal SDK extraction is already implemented and verified.
- Repo-structure refactor is now the active task.

## Status By Milestone
- Milestone 1 in progress: durable-memory refresh for the repo-structure pass.
- Milestone 2 pending: restructure directories and workspace manifests.
- Milestone 3 pending: update references and verify.

## Setup And Verification
- Repo state:
  - `git status --short --branch`
  - `find apps packages -maxdepth 2 -type f | sort`
  - targeted `sed -n` reads of docs, manifests, and key source files
- Validation commands for this pass:
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
  - `rg -n "apps/cli|packages/providers|packages/cli|packages/daemon|packages/tui|packages/core|packages/storage" README.md docs Cargo.toml apps packages`
  - `cargo test --workspace`

## Completed Work
- Established the first-principles product decisions:
  - Gunmetal is one product, not three.
  - Gunmetal App is the local-first product for individuals.
  - An internal Gunmetal SDK powers the app’s multi-provider support.
  - First-party providers are implemented as extensions on top of that SDK.
  - The first built-in toll-booth benefits are request history and token stats.
- Implemented the internal SDK boundary already in the repo:
  - added `packages/sdk`
  - moved provider abstraction/runtime into `packages/sdk`
  - kept first-party provider clients and extension implementations in `packages/providers`
  - rewired CLI, daemon, and TUI to use the SDK-backed provider system

## Validation Results
- The SDK extraction pass was previously verified:
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk` passed
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers` passed
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui` passed
  - `cargo test --workspace` passed
- The repo-structure pass is not implemented yet in this document snapshot.

## Decisions
- Product promise: use AI subscriptions and provider accounts through one local middle layer for inference.
- Gunmetal’s current OpenAI-compatible surface is the initial compatibility layer, not the permanent only abstraction.
- The SDK stays internal until the app architecture is tightly packed and stable.
- UX should stay extremely simple for normal users: connect providers, mint a key, make requests.
- Repo layout should communicate architecture directly:
  - app entrypoint under `apps/gunmetal`
  - SDK under `packages/sdk`
  - first-party extensions under `packages/extensions`
  - app-only Rust support packages under `packages/app-*`

## Next Steps
- Move directories and update workspace manifests to the chosen layout.
- Update path-specific docs and manifest references.
- Re-run the full workspace verification.

## Follow-Ups
- Later work should decide how much of the SDK becomes public API and how much remains internal.
- After the layout is stable, the next substantive phase should focus on tightening the CLI, TUI, and web/browser UX.

Now the repo layout should make that split obvious: Gunmetal App as the product, Gunmetal SDK as the engine, and first-party extensions as the provider implementation layer.

## Core Goal

## Goals

- Reorganize directories so the app, SDK, and extensions are clearly separated.
- Keep crate/package identities stable where practical while changing paths and workspace structure.
- Preserve behavior and keep the workspace green after the moves.

## Non-Goals
- Do not redesign the user-facing UX in this pass.
- Do not publish the SDK yet.
- Do not change runtime behavior beyond what path/reference updates require.
- Do not split this into multiple repos.

## Constraints

- Verify the work before reporting back when possible.
- Use the durable-memory files as the source of truth for subsequent work in this repo.
- Keep the product framing simple for users: user-facing noun is `provider`, internal noun is `extension`.
- The product is local-first and individual-first.
- Gunmetal remains a single product, not multiple separate apps/repos.
- Favor directory/package moves over semantic renames of crate imports unless needed.

## Deliverables

payload.aggregated_output:
# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.

payload.aggregated_output:
# gunmetal

Local-first AI switchboard.

Connect providers you already use, create local API keys, point your apps at `http://127.0.0.1:4684/v1`.

## Install

```bash
npm i -g @dhruv2mars/gunmetal
```

Install downloads the native `gunmetal` binary into `~/.gunmetal/bin/`.

## Quickstart

```bash
gunmetal setup
gunmetal web
gunmetal start
gunmetal status
```

`gunmetal setup` is the golden path. It saves one profile, checks auth, syncs models, creates one key, and ends with a ready-to-run test command.

`gunmetal web` opens the local browser surface at `http://127.0.0.1:4684/app`. `gunmetal start` keeps the local OpenAI-compatible API running at `http://127.0.0.1:4684/v1`.

## Start Here

1. Install: `npm i -g @dhruv2mars/gunmetal`
2. Run `gunmetal setup`
3. Run `gunmetal web` for the local browser UI, or `gunmetal start` for the API only
4. Open `http://127.0.0.1:4684/app` if you want the local control plane
5. Call `GET /v1/models`
6. Call `POST /v1/chat/completions`

```bash
export OPENAI_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:4684/v1
export OPENAI_API_KEY=gm_your_local_key

curl $OPENAI_BASE_URL/models \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY"

curl $OPENAI_BASE_URL/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "codex/gpt-5.4",
    "messages": [{"role":"user","content":"say ok"}]
  }'
```

Then configure any OpenAI-compatible app:

| Setting  | Value                           |
| -------- | ------------------------------- |
| Base URL | `http://127.0.0.1:4684/v1`      |
| API Key  | your gunmetal key               |
| Model    | `openai/gpt-5.1`, `codex/gpt-5.4`, etc. |

## Providers

| Type         | Providers              |
| ------------ | ---------------------- |
| Subscription | `codex`, `copilot`     |
| Gateway      | `openrouter`, `zen`    |
| Direct       | `openai`               |

## API

```
GET  /v1/models
POST /v1/chat/completions
POST /v1/responses
```

Streaming supported on both POST endpoints.

Gunmetal is a normalized gateway by default.

- normalized mode keeps one clean contract across providers
- passthrough mode is opt-in through `gunmetal.mode = "passthrough"` plus `provider_options`
- benchmarks should use normalized mode unless you explicitly want provider-native behavior

Gunmetal works when the app talks to Gunmetal:

- app must let you set a custom base URL
- app must let you send a custom API key
- app must accept arbitrary model ids like `provider/model`
- if it hardcodes the upstream endpoint, Gunmetal cannot help there

## Commands

```bash
gunmetal setup
gunmetal web
gunmetal start
gunmetal status
gunmetal profiles list
gunmetal auth status <profile>
gunmetal models sync <profile>
gunmetal keys list
gunmetal logs list
```

## Structure

```
apps/gunmetal/      # native CLI/TUI entrypoint
apps/web/           # landing page, docs
packages/sdk/       # internal SDK powering provider extensions
packages/sdk-core/  # shared SDK-facing types + contracts
packages/extensions/# first-party provider extensions
packages/app-cli/   # CLI command layer
packages/app-daemon/# local OpenAI-compatible API server
packages/app-storage/# sqlite + local state
packages/app-tui/   # terminal UI
packages/npm/       # npm install wrapper for the native binary
```

## Development

```bash
bun install
bun run dev      # start web dev server
bun run test     # repo structure + all tests
bun run check    # lint + fmt + clippy
cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help
```

## License

MIT

The internal SDK lives in `packages/sdk/`, shared SDK-facing types live in `packages/sdk-core/`, and first-party provider extensions live in `packages/extensions/`. App-only Rust packages live under `packages/app-*`: `packages/app-cli` handles commands, `packages/app-daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/app-storage` owns SQLite/local state, and `packages/app-tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
# Rules
- Be extremely concise.
- Verify work before reporting back when possible.
- Only stop early when truly blocked.
- If the project already has `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/implement.md`, and `docs/documentation.md`, read them before substantial work and keep them current.
- If the user explicitly asks to create durable memory for a project, create or refresh those files in the project's `docs/` folder before coding, then use them as the source of truth for the rest of the task.

See local guidance when relevant:
- [writing.md](~/.codex/docs/writing.md): how to write replies, plans, and questions.
- [testing.md](~/.codex/docs/testing.md): how to approach tests and edge cases.
- [tooling.md](~/.codex/docs/tooling.md): preferred tools for package management and browser checks.
- [version-control.md](~/.codex/docs/version-control.md): branching, pull requests, commits, and ignore-file habits.
- [execution.md](~/.codex/docs/execution.md): how to define done, verify work, and retry before returning.
- [durable-memory.md](~/.codex/docs/durable-memory.md): when to create durable project memory and how to use it.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`apps/cli/` is the native Gunmetal app entrypoint; `apps/web/` holds the Next.js site and docs. Internal Rust modules live in `packages/`: `packages/cli` handles commands, `packages/daemon` serves HTTP, `packages/providers` integrates upstreams, `packages/storage` owns SQLite, `packages/core` shares types, and `packages/tui` renders the terminal UI. `packages/npm/` is the npm distribution wrapper; executable code is in `packages/npm/bin`, tests in `packages/npm/test`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
Use Bun at the repo root.

- `bun install` installs JS workspace deps.
- `bun run dev` starts the web app locally.
- `bun run build` builds web, npm wrapper, and Rust workspace.
- `bun run test` runs the repo-structure guard, web type checks, npm wrapper tests, and `cargo test --workspace`.
- `bun run check` runs web lint, CLI checks, `cargo fmt --check`, and strict Clippy.
- `cargo run -p gunmetal -- --help` smoke-tests the Rust entrypoint.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Rust follows `rustfmt` defaults and `snake_case` module names. Keep types `PascalCase` and prefer small focused modules. TS/JS/TSX uses 2-space indentation, semicolons, and `PascalCase` React components; route files stay in Next.js app-router form such as `src/app/install/page.tsx`. Run `bun run fmt:web` for web fixes and `cargo fmt --all` for Rust. If working in `apps/web`, read `apps/web/AGENTS.md` first; it has extra Next.js-specific rules.

## Testing Guidelines
Follow test-first work. Start with a failing test, then implement. npm wrapper changes should begin with `packages/npm/test/*.test.js`; Rust changes should add or extend nearby `#[cfg(test)]` modules. Run `bun run test` before opening a PR. Target full coverage for touched code; do not merge untested paths.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Create a branch for every change. Keep commits atomic and use prefixes seen in history: `feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`. Use GitHub CLI for repo flow: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`. PRs should say what changed, why, how it was verified, and include screenshots for web UI changes. Merge to `main`, then delete the branch.

## Security & Configuration Tips
Do not commit secrets. `.env*`, `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, and `target/` are ignored already; keep local runtime state there or under temp files, not in tracked source.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

# Prompt

## Task
- Continue the first-principles refactor by restructuring the repository itself around the new architecture. The SDK boundary already exists. Now the repo layout should make that split obvious: Gunmetal App as the product, Gunmetal SDK as the engine, and first-party extensions as the provider implementation layer.

## Core Goal
- Make the repository structure match the product architecture so future SDK publication and app work both fit naturally.

## Goals
- Update durable memory for the repo-structure refactor scope.
- Reorganize directories so the app, SDK, and extensions are clearly separated.
- Keep crate/package identities stable where practical while changing paths and workspace structure.
- Preserve behavior and keep the workspace green after the moves.

## Non-Goals
- Do not redesign the user-facing UX in this pass.
- Do not publish the SDK yet.
- Do not change runtime behavior beyond what path/reference updates require.
- Do not split this into multiple repos.

## Constraints
- Be concise.
- Verify the work before reporting back when possible.
- Use the durable-memory files as the source of truth for subsequent work in this repo.
- Keep the product framing simple for users: user-facing noun is `provider`, internal noun is `extension`.
- The product is local-first and individual-first.
- Gunmetal remains a single product, not multiple separate apps/repos.
- Favor directory/package moves over semantic renames of crate imports unless needed.

## Deliverables
- Updated durable-memory docs for the repo-structure pass
- Reorganized workspace directories and manifest paths
- Updated docs/build references to the new layout
- Verification notes for the restructure

## Process Requirements
- Update durable memory before broad implementation.
- Work in two phases:
  - phase 1: durable-memory refresh
  - phase 2: directory/workspace restructure and reference updates
- Keep the docs current as architecture decisions land.

## Done When
- Durable memory reflects the repo-structure decisions.
- The repo tree clearly separates app packages, SDK, and extensions.
- Workspace manifests and docs point to the new layout.
- The repo still builds/tests for the touched areas.

# Plans

This document is the execution plan and ongoing notes for the current task. Keep it current as the work moves.

## Verification Checklist
- `sed -n '1,220p' docs/prompt.md`
- `sed -n '1,260p' docs/plans.md`
- `sed -n '1,260p' docs/documentation.md`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
- `rg -n "apps/cli|packages/providers|packages/cli|packages/daemon|packages/tui|packages/core|packages/storage" README.md AGENTS.md Cargo.toml test apps packages`
  - expect no matches after the restructure is complete
- `cargo test --workspace`
- Last verified: 2026-04-12

## Milestones
- `[done]` Milestone 1: refresh durable memory for the repo-structure pass
  - Scope: rewrite the active task around repo layout rather than SDK extraction
  - Key areas: `docs/prompt.md`, `docs/plans.md`, `docs/documentation.md`
  - Acceptance criteria: docs reflect the chosen target layout and why it exists
  - Verification steps: direct review of the updated docs
- `[done]` Milestone 2: restructure directories and workspace manifests
  - Scope: move app-facing directories and extension directories into the new layout
  - Key areas: `apps/*`, `packages/*`, root `Cargo.toml`, crate-local `Cargo.toml`
  - Acceptance criteria: the tree reads clearly as app vs sdk vs extensions while crate names remain stable
  - Verification steps: workspace manifest review and targeted cargo tests
- `[done]` Milestone 3: update references and verify
  - Scope: update docs/build/code references to the moved paths and re-run validation
  - Key areas: `README.md`, root docs, manifests, any path-specific references
  - Acceptance criteria: no stale path references remain in core docs/manifests and the workspace tests still pass
  - Verification steps: `rg` for old paths plus `cargo test --workspace`

## Acceptance Checks
- Durable memory states the repo-structure decisions for app vs sdk vs extensions.
- The repo tree makes the SDK/app split obvious.
- Old path references are removed or intentionally kept only where required.
- The workspace still tests green after the restructure.

## Validation
- `git status --short --branch`
- targeted `sed -n` reads across docs and provider/runtime source files
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
- `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
- `rg -n "apps/cli|packages/providers|packages/cli|packages/daemon|packages/tui|packages/core|packages/storage" README.md AGENTS.md Cargo.toml test apps packages`
  - expect no matches after the restructure is complete
- `cargo test --workspace`

## Decisions
- Product thesis: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer that turns AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.
- User-facing noun is `provider`; internal implementation noun is `extension`.
- The app is the product. The SDK is internal first and powers the app.
- Request history and token stats are the first built-in toll-booth benefits to preserve and strengthen later.
- The current pass is repo-structure-first, not UX-polish-first.
- Target layout:
  - `apps/gunmetal` for the native app entrypoint
  - `apps/web` for the hosted site/docs
  - `packages/sdk` for the engine
  - `packages/extensions` for first-party provider extensions
  - `packages/app-*` for app-only internal Rust packages

## Implementation Notes
- Current branch for this work: `refactor/internal-sdk`.
- The SDK boundary already exists and is green.
- This pass is mainly path/layout churn with manifest updates.
- Keep crate names stable where possible to avoid unnecessary import renames.
- Implemented layout:
  - `apps/gunmetal`
  - `packages/sdk`
  - `packages/sdk-core`
  - `packages/extensions`
  - `packages/app-cli`
  - `packages/app-daemon`
  - `packages/app-storage`
  - `packages/app-tui`
- Verification completed:
  - `node --test test/repo-structure.test.js`
  - `bun run test`

## Risks
- Risk: broad directory moves create stale manifest and doc references.
  - Mitigation: use a targeted `rg` sweep for old paths and rerun the full workspace tests.
- Risk: refactor accidentally changes runtime behavior.
  - Mitigation: preserve crate names and current dependency semantics while only changing layout.
- Risk: the new tree optimizes for theory over current use.
  - Mitigation: keep the layout minimal and directly tied to the existing app/sdk/extensions split.

## Architecture
- Product: local-first inference middle layer for individuals.
- Canonical flow: `app/tool -> Gunmetal key -> Gunmetal -> provider extension -> upstream provider`.
- Core product nouns:
  - provider accounts
  - models
  - Gunmetal keys
  - requests
  - usage stats
- Internal architecture target:
  - inbound compatibility layer
  - Gunmetal core request/control layer
  - Gunmetal SDK for provider extensions
  - first-party provider extensions

# Documentation

This document is updated continuously as milestones land so it reflects reality.

## What This Work Is
- This pass continues the first-principles refactor by aligning the repository layout with the new architecture. The SDK boundary already exists. Now the tree itself should clearly show Gunmetal App, Gunmetal SDK, and first-party extensions as separate concerns.

## Current Status
- Durable memory exists and is updated for the repo-structure pass.
- Product thesis: Gunmetal is the local-first inference middle layer that turns AI subscriptions and provider accounts into one programmable API.
- User-facing term is `provider`; internal implementation term is `extension`.
- Current branch for this refactor: `refactor/internal-sdk`.
- The internal SDK extraction is already implemented and verified.
- Repo-structure refactor is implemented and verified.

## Status By Milestone
- Milestone 1 complete: durable-memory refresh for the repo-structure pass.
- Milestone 2 complete: restructured directories and workspace manifests.
- Milestone 3 complete: updated references and verified the repo.

## Setup And Verification
- Repo state:
  - `git status --short --branch`
  - `find apps packages -maxdepth 2 -type f | sort`
  - targeted `sed -n` reads of docs, manifests, and key source files
- Validation commands for this pass:
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
  - `rg -n "apps/cli|packages/providers|packages/cli|packages/daemon|packages/tui|packages/core|packages/storage" README.md AGENTS.md Cargo.toml test apps packages`
    expecting no matches after the restructure is complete
  - `cargo test --workspace`

## Completed Work
- Established the first-principles product decisions:
  - Gunmetal is one product, not three.
  - Gunmetal App is the local-first product for individuals.
  - An internal Gunmetal SDK powers the app’s multi-provider support.
  - First-party providers are implemented as extensions on top of that SDK.
  - The first built-in toll-booth benefits are request history and token stats.
- Implemented the internal SDK boundary already in the repo:
  - added `packages/sdk`
  - moved provider abstraction/runtime into `packages/sdk`
  - kept first-party provider clients and extension implementations in `packages/extensions`
  - rewired CLI, daemon, and TUI to use the SDK-backed provider system
- Restructured the repo layout to match the architecture:
  - `apps/gunmetal` now holds the native app entrypoint
  - `packages/extensions` holds first-party provider extensions
  - `packages/sdk-core` holds shared SDK-facing types
  - app-only Rust support crates now live under `packages/app-*`

## Validation Results
- The SDK extraction pass was previously verified:
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk` passed
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers` passed
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui` passed
  - `cargo test --workspace` passed
- Repo-structure verification also passed:
  - `node --test test/repo-structure.test.js` passed
  - `bun run test` passed
  - legacy path sweep across `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `Cargo.toml`, `test`, `apps`, and `packages` returned no stale old-layout matches

## Decisions
- Product promise: use AI subscriptions and provider accounts through one local middle layer for inference.
- Gunmetal’s current OpenAI-compatible surface is the initial compatibility layer, not the permanent only abstraction.
- The SDK stays internal until the app architecture is tightly packed and stable.
- UX should stay extremely simple for normal users: connect providers, mint a key, make requests.
- Repo layout should communicate architecture directly:
  - app entrypoint under `apps/gunmetal`
  - SDK under `packages/sdk`
  - first-party extensions under `packages/extensions`
  - app-only Rust support packages under `packages/app-*`

## Next Steps
- Use the new repo layout as the base for the next phase: simplify and tighten the Gunmetal app UX.
- Revisit naming and public packaging only when the internal SDK surface is stable enough to publish confidently.

## Follow-Ups
- Later work should decide how much of the SDK becomes public API and how much remains internal.
- After the layout is stable, the next substantive phase should focus on tightening the CLI, TUI, and web/browser UX.

## Status By Milestone
- Milestone 1 complete: durable-memory refresh for the repo-structure pass.
- Milestone 2 complete: restructured directories and workspace manifests.
- Milestone 3 complete: updated references and verified the repo.

## Setup And Verification
- Repo state:
  - `git status --short --branch`
  - `find apps packages -maxdepth 2 -type f | sort`
  - targeted `sed -n` reads of docs, manifests, and key source files
- Validation commands for this pass:
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk`
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers`
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui`
  - `rg -n "apps/cli|packages/providers|packages/cli|packages/daemon|packages/tui|packages/core|packages/storage" README.md AGENTS.md Cargo.toml test apps packages`
    expecting no matches after the restructure is complete
  - `cargo test --workspace`

## Completed Work
- Established the first-principles product decisions:
  - Gunmetal is one product, not three.
  - Gunmetal App is the local-first product for individuals.
  - An internal Gunmetal SDK powers the app’s multi-provider support.
  - First-party providers are implemented as extensions on top of that SDK.
  - The first built-in toll-booth benefits are request history and token stats.
- Implemented the internal SDK boundary already in the repo:
  - added `packages/sdk`
  - moved provider abstraction/runtime into `packages/sdk`
  - kept first-party provider clients and extension implementations in `packages/extensions`
  - rewired CLI, daemon, and TUI to use the SDK-backed provider system
- Restructured the repo layout to match the architecture:
  - `apps/gunmetal` now holds the native app entrypoint
  - `packages/extensions` holds first-party provider extensions
  - `packages/sdk-core` holds shared SDK-facing types
  - app-only Rust support crates now live under `packages/app-*`

## Validation Results
- The SDK extraction pass was previously verified:
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-sdk` passed
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-providers` passed
  - `cargo test -p gunmetal-cli -p gunmetal-daemon -p gunmetal-tui` passed
  - `cargo test --workspace` passed
- Repo-structure verification also passed:
  - `node --test test/repo-structure.test.js` passed
  - `bun run test` passed
  - legacy path sweep across `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `Cargo.toml`, `test`, `apps`, and `packages` returned no stale old-layout matches

## Decisions
- Product promise: use AI subscriptions and provider accounts through one local middle layer for inference.
- Gunmetal’s current OpenAI-compatible surface is the initial compatibility layer, not the permanent only abstraction.
- The SDK stays internal until the app architecture is tightly packed and stable.
- UX should stay extremely simple for normal users: connect providers, mint a key, make requests.
- Repo layout should communicate architecture directly:
  - app entrypoint under `apps/gunmetal`
  - SDK under `packages/sdk`
  - first-party extensions under `packages/extensions`
  - app-only Rust support packages under `packages/app-*`

## Next Steps
- Use the new repo layout as the base for the next phase: simplify and tighten the Gunmetal app UX.
- Revisit naming and public packaging only when the internal SDK surface is stable enough to publish confidently.

## Follow-Ups
- Later work should decide how much of the SDK becomes public API and how much remains internal.
- After the layout is stable, the next substantive phase should focus on tightening the CLI, TUI, and web/browser UX.

---
name: frontend-design
description: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
license: Complete terms in LICENSE.txt
---

This skill guides creation of distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces that avoid generic "AI slop" aesthetics. Implement real working code with exceptional attention to aesthetic details and creative choices.

The user provides frontend requirements: a component, page, application, or interface to build. They may include context about the purpose, audience, or technical constraints.

## Design Thinking

Before coding, understand the context and commit to a BOLD aesthetic direction:
- **Purpose**: What problem does this interface solve? Who uses it?
- **Tone**: Pick an extreme: brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian, etc. There are so many flavors to choose from. Use these for inspiration but design one that is true to the aesthetic direction.
- **Constraints**: Technical requirements (framework, performance, accessibility).
- **Differentiation**: What makes this UNFORGETTABLE? What's the one thing someone will remember?

**CRITICAL**: Choose a clear conceptual direction and execute it with precision. Bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work - the key is intentionality, not intensity.

Then implement working code (HTML/CSS/JS, React, Vue, etc.) that is:
- Production-grade and functional
- Visually striking and memorable
- Cohesive with a clear aesthetic point-of-view
- Meticulously refined in every detail

## Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines

Focus on:
- **Typography**: Choose fonts that are beautiful, unique, and interesting. Avoid generic fonts like Arial and Inter; opt instead for distinctive choices that elevate the frontend's aesthetics; unexpected, characterful font choices. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.
- **Color & Theme**: Commit to a cohesive aesthetic. Use CSS variables for consistency. Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.
- **Motion**: Use animations for effects and micro-interactions. Prioritize CSS-only solutions for HTML. Use Motion library for React when available. Focus on high-impact moments: one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals (animation-delay) creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions. Use scroll-triggering and hover states that surprise.
- **Spatial Composition**: Unexpected layouts. Asymmetry. Overlap. Diagonal flow. Grid-breaking elements. Generous negative space OR controlled density.
- **Backgrounds & Visual Details**: Create atmosphere and depth rather than defaulting to solid colors. Add contextual effects and textures that match the overall aesthetic. Apply creative forms like gradient meshes, noise textures, geometric patterns, layered transparencies, dramatic shadows, decorative borders, custom cursors, and grain overlays.

NEVER use generic AI-generated aesthetics like overused font families (Inter, Roboto, Arial, system fonts), cliched color schemes (particularly purple gradients on white backgrounds), predictable layouts and component patterns, and cookie-cutter design that lacks context-specific character.

Interpret creatively and make unexpected choices that feel genuinely designed for the context. No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes, different fonts, different aesthetics. NEVER converge on common choices (Space Grotesk, for example) across generations.

**IMPORTANT**: Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision. Maximalist designs need elaborate code with extensive animations and effects. Minimalist or refined designs need restraint, precision, and careful attention to spacing, typography, and subtle details. Elegance comes from executing the vision well.

Remember: Claude is capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back, show what can truly be created when thinking outside the box and committing fully to a distinctive vision.

<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8" />
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
    <title>Gunmetal Web</title>
    <style>
      :root {
        color-scheme: dark;
        --bg: #060708;
        --panel: rgba(19, 22, 26, 0.88);
        --panel-2: rgba(12, 15, 18, 0.94);
        --line: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1);
        --line-strong: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.18);
        --text: #f4f4ee;
        --muted: #9aa3ad;
        --accent: #d8b26e;
        --accent-2: #7dd3c8;
        --danger: #ff8d78;
        --shadow: 0 24px 80px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.45);
        --radius: 24px;
      }

      * {
        box-sizing: border-box;
      }

      body {
        margin: 0;
        min-height: 100vh;
        font-family: "IBM Plex Sans", "SF Pro Display", system-ui, sans-serif;
        color: var(--text);
        background:
          radial-gradient(circle at top left, rgba(216, 178, 110, 0.16), transparent 34%),
          radial-gradient(circle at top right, rgba(125, 211, 200, 0.12), transparent 28%),
          linear-gradient(180deg, #0d1013, var(--bg) 34%);
      }

      a {
        color: inherit;
      }

      button,
      input,
      select {
        font: inherit;
      }

      .shell {
        width: min(1480px, calc(100vw - 32px));
        margin: 0 auto;
        padding: 20px 0 56px;
      }

      .masthead {
        display: grid;
        gap: 20px;
        padding: 20px 0 28px;
      }

      .brand-row,
      .status-row,
      .command-row,
      .section-grid {
        display: grid;
        gap: 16px;
      }

      .brand-row {
        grid-template-columns: minmax(0, 1.35fr) minmax(360px, 0.65fr);
      }

      .hero,
      .hero-side,
      .panel {
        border: 1px solid var(--line);
        border-radius: var(--radius);
        background: linear-gradient(180deg, var(--panel), var(--panel-2));
        box-shadow: var(--shadow);
        backdrop-filter: blur(18px);
      }

      .hero {
        position: relative;
        overflow: hidden;
        padding: 28px;
      }

      .hero::before {
        content: "";
        position: absolute;
        inset: 0;
        background:
          linear-gradient(120deg, rgba(216, 178, 110, 0.16), transparent 32%),
          radial-gradient(circle at 80% 10%, rgba(125, 211, 200, 0.16), transparent 24%);
        pointer-events: none;
      }

      .eyebrow {
        font-size: 12px;
        letter-spacing: 0.24em;
        text-transform: uppercase;
        color: var(--accent);
        margin: 0 0 12px;
      }

      h1,
      h2,
      h3,
      p {
        margin: 0;
      }

      h1 {
        max-width: 11ch;
        font-size: clamp(3rem, 8vw, 6.4rem);
        line-height: 0.9;
        letter-spacing: -0.04em;
      }

      .lede {
        max-width: 34rem;
        margin-top: 16px;
        font-size: clamp(1rem, 2vw, 1.18rem);
        line-height: 1.45;
        color: rgba(244, 244, 238, 0.84);
      }

      .hero-actions,
      .mini-actions,
      .provider-list,
      .count-grid {
        display: flex;
        flex-wrap: wrap;
        gap: 10px;
      }

      .hero-actions {
        margin-top: 22px;
      }

      .btn,
      .ghost-btn {
        border: 1px solid var(--line-strong);
        border-radius: 999px;
        padding: 10px 14px;
        color: var(--text);
        background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.04);
        cursor: pointer;
        transition: transform 0.16s ease, border-color 0.16s ease, background 0.16s ease;
      }

      .btn:hover,
      .ghost-btn:hover {
        transform: translateY(-1px);
        border-color: rgba(216, 178, 110, 0.58);
      }

      .btn.primary {
        background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(216, 178, 110, 0.2), rgba(216, 178, 110, 0.08));
        border-color: rgba(216, 178, 110, 0.48);
      }

      .hero-side,
      .panel {
        padding: 20px;
      }

      .hero-side {
        display: grid;
        gap: 14px;
        align-content: start;
      }

      .status-row {
        grid-template-columns: repeat(4, minmax(0, 1fr));
      }

      .stat {
        padding: 18px;
        border: 1px solid var(--line);
        border-radius: 20px;
        background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.02);
      }

      .stat-label {
        font-size: 12px;
        text-transform: uppercase;
        letter-spacing: 0.18em;
        color: var(--muted);
      }

      .stat-value {
        margin-top: 8px;
        font-size: clamp(1.8rem, 3.6vw, 3rem);
        line-height: 0.94;
      }

      .command-row {
        grid-template-columns: minmax(0, 1.2fr) minmax(320px, 0.8fr);
      }

      .panel-kicker {
        font-size: 11px;
        text-transform: uppercase;
        letter-spacing: 0.22em;
        color: var(--muted);
      }

      .mono {
        font-family: "IBM Plex Mono", "SFMono-Regular", ui-monospace, monospace;
      }

      .command-block {
        margin-top: 14px;
        padding: 14px;
        border-radius: 18px;
        border: 1px solid var(--line);
        background: rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.26);
        overflow: auto;
      }

      .section-grid {
        grid-template-columns: minmax(0, 0.86fr) minmax(340px, 0.74fr);
        align-items: start;
      }

      .stack {
        display: grid;
        gap: 16px;
      }

      .profile-grid {
        display: grid;
        gap: 14px;
      }

      .profile-card {
        border: 1px solid var(--line);
        border-radius: 22px;
        padding: 18px;
        background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.02);
      }

      .profile-meta,
      .key-meta {
        display: flex;
        flex-wrap: wrap;
        gap: 10px;
        margin-top: 10px;
      }

      .pill {
        border-radius: 999px;
        padding: 6px 10px;
        background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.04);
        border: 1px solid var(--line);
        font-size: 13px;
        color: var(--muted);
      }

Install + setup

One install. One setup flow.

shell

First request

Confirm /v1/models, then /v1/chat/completions.

payload.message: I’m keeping this first UX pass disciplined: terminology, first-run framing, and the browser/TUI surfaces that a normal user sees immediately. I’m reading the remaining shared web shells and the key CLI strings before editing so the pass stays consistent.

import type { Metadata } from "next"; import { SiteShell } from "@/components/site-shell"; export const metadata: Metadata = { title: "Web UI", }; const steps = [ "Install Gunmetal globally with npm.", "Run `gunmetal setup` once to create a profile and a local key.", "Run `gunmetal web` to start the local browser surface and open it in your browser.", "Use the same machine-local UI to auth providers, sync models, create keys, and inspect logs.", ]; export default function WebUiPage() { return (

Launch

One command opens the local control plane.

The Web UI is not a hosted dashboard. It is a local surface, served by Gunmetal itself, so profiles, sessions, keys, and logs stay on the same machine at `127.0.0.1`.

    {steps.map((step) => (
  1. {step}
    2. ))}
browser flow 
{`npm i -g @dhruv2mars/gunmetal

gunmetal setup
gunmetal web

# local web surface
http://127.0.0.1:4684/app

# local API surface
http://127.0.0.1:4684/v1`}

What it does

  • create provider profiles
  • open browser auth for codex and copilot
  • save direct API keys for gateway and direct providers
  • sync model catalogs
  • create, disable, revoke, and delete Gunmetal keys
  • inspect recent request logs

Design rule

The browser surface is meant to operate Gunmetal, not explain it. The public landing page can stay polished and hosted. The Web UI stays local, fast, and close to the machine that owns the secrets.

); }

content.text:
pre-requisits:
- you need to checkout the whole codebase, git repo, full history of work progress that have happened. this is so that you have full context about what it is, my vision, how things are working, and what has happened till now. 
- you need to create durable-memory for this project. 
- if you have any questions, doubts, need more context, ask me.


once the is done. we will move forward!

Durable memory is initialized and verified. I reviewed the current repo shape plus the visible git history, including the main product arc from foundation/provider rollout through web UI parity, npm distribution, and the later stabilization/release cycles up to `v0.1.8`.
